title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: psvr R package implementing four SVR models derived unified mathematical framework percentage-error loss functions. targets forecasting contexts targets yk > 0 strictly positive relative accuracy matters absolute accuracy. Source paper: Unified Family Percentage-Error Support Vector Regression Models Symmetric Kernel Extensions (MDPI Mathematics, 2026). Full derivations paper.tex repository root.

what: Model 1 — ε-SVR with MAPE (Theorem 1 / Appendix A.1)
title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: Primal: Dual QP (max form): βk = αk - αk*, |βk| = αk + αk*. KKT: ω = Σk βk φ(xk), Σk βk = 0, 0 ≤ αk, αk* ≤ 100C/yk. Prediction: f(x) = Σk βk K(xk, x) + b Implementation: QP via osqp. Key files: R/mape_svr.R.
code: min  ½‖ω‖² + C Σk (ξk + ξk*) s.t. (100/yk)(yk - ωᵀφ(xk) - b) ≤ ε + ξk      (100/yk)(ωᵀφ(xk) + b - yk) ≤ ε + ξk*      ξk, ξk* ≥ 0 max  -½ Σk,l βk βl K(xk,xl) + Σk βk yk - (ε/100) Σk |βk| yk s.t. Σk βk = 0      |βk| ≤ 100C / yk    (sample-dependent box; yk small → tight bound)

what: Model 2 — Symmetric ε-SVR with MAPE (Theorem 2 / Appendix A.2)
title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: primal Model 1, augmented symmetry constraint: ωᵀφ(xk) = · ωᵀφ(-xk) Dual QP (max form): Ks(xi,xj) = K(xi,xj) + ·K(xi,-xj). factor ¼ (vs ½ Model 1) arises symmetrization representer theorem (Appendix .2): ωᵀφ(xk) = ½ Σl βl Ks(xl, xk), quadratic term picks extra ½. Prediction: f(x) = ½ Σk βk Ks(xk, x) + b Implementation: QP via osqp. Build Ks = Ω + ·Ω* inline (½), pass P = ¼·Ks QP quadratic matrix — ¼ factor absorbed P directly. use sym_kernel_matrix() ; helper returns ½(Ω + ·Ω*) = Ωs, correct Model 4. Key files: R/mape_sym_svr.R.
code: max  -¼ Σk,l βk βl Ks(xk,xl) + Σk βk yk - (ε/100) Σk |βk| yk s.t. Σk βk = 0      |βk| ≤ 100C / yk    (unchanged from Model 1)

what: Model 3 — LS-SVR with RMSPE (Theorem 3 / Appendix A.3)
title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: Primal: KKT stationarity: - ω = Σk αk φ(xk) - Σk αk = 0 - ek = (yk²/Γ) αk Linear system (solve directly): YΓ = diag(y1²/Γ, …, yN²/Γ) — added diagonal Ω . Prediction: f(x) = Σk αk K(xk, x) + b Implementation: base::solve() (N+1)×(N+1) augmented matrix. Key files: R/rmspe_lssvr.R.
code: min  ½‖ω‖² + (Γ/2) Σk ek²/yk² s.t. yk = ωᵀφ(xk) + b + ek [ 0    1ᵀ      ] [b]   [0] [ 1    Ω + YΓ  ] [α] = [y]

what: Model 4 — Symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE (Theorem 4 / Appendix A.4)
title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: primal Model 3, augmented symmetry constraint. Linear system: Ωs = ½(Ω + aΩ*), Ω*kl = K(xk, -xl). Symmetric representer: ωᵀφ(xk) = Σl (Ωs)kl αl Prediction: f(x) = ½ Σk αk Ks(xk, x) + b equivalently: f(x) = Σk αk [K(xk,x) + ·K(xk,-x)] / 2 + b Implementation: base::solve() Ωs replacing Ω. Key files: R/rmspe_sym_lssvr.R.

title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: Required Models 2 4: - K(-xi, xj) = K(xi, -xj) - K(-xi, -xj) = K(xi, xj) RBF kernel K(xi, xj) = exp(-‖xi - xj‖²/2σ²) satisfies conditions: - K(-xi, -xj) = exp(-‖-xi-(-xj)‖²/2σ²) = exp(-‖xi-xj‖²/2σ²) = K(xi,xj) ✓ - K(-xi, xj) = exp(-‖-xi-xj‖²/2σ²) = exp(-‖xi+xj‖²/2σ²) = K(xi,-xj) ✓ Polynomial kernels even degree also satisfy Assumption 3.

title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: QP solver: osqp package (Models 1 & 2). Supports sparse matrices, commercial license. Linear solver: base::solve() (Models 3 & 4). Dense (N+1)×(N+1) system, extra dep. Target validation: fit() functions call stopifnot((y > 0)). Kernel interface: make_kernel(type, ...) returns closure function(xi, xj) type ∈ {"rbf", "linear", "polynomial"}. Shared across four models. YΓ storage: stored vector y^2 / gamma; added diagonal via diag(Omega) <- diag(Omega) + y_gamma — never materializes full N×N diagonal matrix. "psvr_mape" (Model 1) "psvr_mape_sym" (Model 2) "psvr_rmspe" (Model 3) "psvr_rmspe_sym" (Model 4) S3 methods: predict.psvr_*() class. Documentation: roxygen2 @param, @return, @examples. Style: tidyverse / base R style: snake_case, <- assignment. License: MIT.

title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: yk > 0 always — validated fit time, never silently coerced. ∈ {-1, 1} symmetric models. symmetric kernel Ks(xi, xj) = K(xi, xj) + ·K(xi, -xj) uses negation xj, kernel closure must accept negative inputs even training data positive. Prediction Model 4: use ½(K(xk,x) + ·K(xk,-x)) per support vector.

title: Case Study: Boston Housing
text: Boston Housing dataset (Harrison & Rubinfeld, 1978) canonical regression benchmark containing 506 observations across 13 predictors. target variable, MEDV (median home value $1,000s), strictly positive, making natural candidate percentage-error evaluation. Absolute-error metrics MSE treat $2,000 prediction error regardless whether true value $10,000 $100,000. Percentage-error metrics — MAPE RMSPE — penalize error proportionally target scale, better reflects real-estate valuation decisions relative accuracy drives business outcomes. article demonstrates psvr models — trained directly MAPE RMSPE loss — consistently outperform standard baselines native percentage-error metrics 30 randomized 80/20 train/test splits. data loaded mlbench::BostonHousing.

title: Case Study: Boston Housing
text: benchmark follows 30-seed protocol: seed 1–30, 506 observations randomly split 80% training (≈ 405 rows) 20% test (≈ 101 rows). Hyperparameters selected via 5-fold cross-validation training set, minimizing model’s native CV metric. Grid sizes 48 combinations SVR-MAPE Log-SVR; 96 SVR-MAPE+SK (symmetry type ∈ {-1, 1} jointly tuned cost, margin, bandwidth); 16 LS-RMSPE variants ε-SVR (MSE); 32 LS-RMSPE+SK (jointly tuned cost bandwidth); 6 Random Forest; 8 XGBoost; tuning Linear Regression, WLS, Quantile Regression. Six metrics recorded held-test set: MAPE, RMSPE, MAAPE, MASE, MSE, R². Prior fitting, predictor features standardized zero mean unit variance using statistics computed training fold ; test-set features scaled parameters prevent data leakage. MASE uses lag-1 naive denominator; Boston Housing data temporal ordering, denominator depends row order within split interpreted accordingly.

title: Case Study: Boston Housing
text: SVR-MAPE consistently achieves lowest MAPE across splits dual objective directly minimizes weighted absolute-percentage loss; post-hoc transformation required. LS-RMSPE MAPE-based CV tuning (LS-RMSPE-M) ranks closely behind, benefiting closed-form linear system avoids QP solver sensitivity. Models symmetric kernels — SVR-MAPE+SK LS-RMSPE+SK — tend show tighter bootstrap confidence intervals, suggesting reduced variance across 30 splits even mean performance comparable. expected, R² slightly lower psvr models XGBoost Random Forest: optimizing MAPE trades explained variance scale-free accuracy. deliberate design choice flaw — MAPE deployment metric (e.g., property valuation models reported clients percentage terms), psvr models appropriate choice. applications R² MSE drives decisions, ensemble methods remain competitive. Practical recommendation: use SVR-MAPE LS-RMSPE MAPE target deployment metric; prefer XGBoost Random Forest R² matters .

Theorem 5 predicts former.

title: Boston Housing — reflection-augmented 2x2 ablation
text: family, compute per-seed differences relative cell 1 (non-augmented, non-symmetric) report mean ± bootstrap CI. Δ-decomposition (positive = improvement cell 1 baseline).
code: delta_decomposition <- function(results, family_letter) {   fam <- results |>     filter(family == family_letter) |>     select(seed, cell_id, MAPE) |>     pivot_wider(names_from = cell_id, values_from = MAPE)    cells <- if (family_letter == "A") c("A1", "A2", "A3", "A4")            else                       c("B1", "B2", "B3", "B4")   d_aug   <- fam[[cells[1]]] - fam[[cells[2]]]   d_sym   <- fam[[cells[1]]] - fam[[cells[3]]]   d_joint <- fam[[cells[1]]] - fam[[cells[4]]]    fmt <- function(x) {     bs <- bootstrap_ci(x)     sprintf("%+.3f [%+.3f, %+.3f]", bs[["mean"]], bs[["lower"]], bs[["upper"]])   }    tibble::tibble(     family   = family_letter,     delta    = c("Δ_aug (cell1 − cell2)",                  "Δ_sym (cell1 − cell3)",                  "Δ_joint (cell1 − cell4)"),     estimate = c(fmt(d_aug), fmt(d_sym), fmt(d_joint))   ) }  bind_rows(   delta_decomposition(results, "A"),   delta_decomposition(results, "B") ) |>   knitr::kable(     caption = "Δ-decomposition (positive = improvement over cell 1 baseline)."   )

title: Boston Housing — reflection-augmented 2x2 ablation
text: Paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test, non-baseline cell vs cell 1.
code: wilcoxon_per_family <- function(results, family_letter) {   fam <- results |>     filter(family == family_letter) |>     select(seed, cell_id, MAPE) |>     pivot_wider(names_from = cell_id, values_from = MAPE)    baseline <- if (family_letter == "A") "A1" else "B1"   comparisons <- if (family_letter == "A") c("A2", "A3", "A4")                  else                       c("B2", "B3", "B4")    do.call(rbind, lapply(comparisons, function(c2) {     test <- stats::wilcox.test(fam[[baseline]], fam[[c2]],                                paired = TRUE, exact = FALSE)     tibble::tibble(       family    = family_letter,       cell      = c2,       reference = baseline,       mean_diff = mean(fam[[baseline]] - fam[[c2]], na.rm = TRUE),       p_value   = test$p.value,       signif    = case_when(         test$p.value < 0.001 ~ "***",         test$p.value < 0.01  ~ "**",         test$p.value < 0.05  ~ "*",         TRUE                 ~ "ns"       )     )   })) }  bind_rows(   wilcoxon_per_family(results, "A"),   wilcoxon_per_family(results, "B") ) |>   mutate(     mean_diff = sprintf("%+.3f", mean_diff),     p_value   = sprintf("%.4f",  p_value)   ) |>   knitr::kable(     caption = "Paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test, each non-baseline cell vs cell 1."   )

title: Boston Housing — reflection-augmented 2x2 ablation
text: Δ-decomposition table empirical answer question “symmetry come kernel data?”: Δ_sym dominates Δ_aug → kernel constraint load-bearing contribution. Theorem 5 predicts. Δ_aug dominates Δ_sym → data augmentation alone sufficient; kernel-level constraint adds little. Theorem 5 still hold formally (dual problems equivalent optimum), finite-sample / finite-budget statistical behavior favor augmentation practical lever. Δ_aug ≈ Δ_sym ≈ Δ_joint / 2 → two effects additive roughly equal. useful; neither dominates. Δ_joint approximately Δ_sym + Δ_aug minus interaction term. Negative interaction (Δ_joint < Δ_sym + Δ_aug) expected: applying symmetric kernel already-augmented data partially redundant, since symmetric kernel already encodes f(x) = f(-x) constraint augmentation enforces statistically.

title: Case Study: Diabetes Progression
text: benchmark follows 30-seed protocol: seed 1–30, 442 observations randomly split 80% training (≈ 353 rows) 20% test (≈ 89 rows). Hyperparameters selected via 5-fold cross-validation training set, minimizing model’s native CV metric. Grid sizes 48 combinations SVR-MAPE Log-SVR; 96 SVR-MAPE+SK (symmetry type ∈ {-1, 1} jointly tuned cost, margin, bandwidth); 16 LS-RMSPE variants ε-SVR (MSE); 32 LS-RMSPE+SK (jointly tuned cost bandwidth); 6 Random Forest; 8 XGBoost; tuning Linear Regression, WLS, Quantile Regression. Six metrics recorded held-test set: MAPE, RMSPE, MAAPE, MASE, MSE, R². Prior fitting, predictor features standardized zero mean unit variance using statistics computed training fold ; test-set features scaled parameters prevent data leakage. MASE uses lag-1 naive denominator; Diabetes data temporal ordering, denominator depends row order within split interpreted accordingly.

text: SVR-MAPE leads MAPE (34–35% range) despite dataset’s high CV; predicting always mean yields approximately 62% MAPE, psvr models capture meaningful signal. R² modest (0.3–0.5) models — expected small, noisy dataset linear methods competitive. Symmetric kernel models (m2, m4a, m4b) show R² near zero negative: Diabetes natural symmetry structure, symmetric kernel term introduces noise rather useful inductive bias. delimits applicability symmetric extensions datasets inherent symmetry feature space. Boston Housing, optimizing MAPE trades explained variance scale-free accuracy. SVR-MAPE appropriate model percentage-error clinical reporting metric. R²-focused tasks, linear regression remains competitive small dataset considered strong baseline. Practical recommendation: use SVR-MAPE percentage-error clinical reporting metric; R²-focused tasks, linear regression remains competitive dataset.

title: Case Study: Victorian Electricity Demand
text: Electricity demand forecasting canonical application relative accuracy—measured Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)—carries direct operational significance. Victoria, Australia operates competitive spot market generators must submit binding supply offers one day ahead; 5% forecast error 200 MWh peak day 5% error 80 MWh -peak day proportionally equivalent implications reserve capacity planning dispatch scheduling. Absolute-error objectives MSE MAE respect symmetry, motivating loss functions explicitly penalise relative deviations. four psvr models studied derived percentage-error loss functions—MAPE RMSPE—provide principled alternative standard SVR setting. additional structural feature daily electricity demand motivates exploration symmetric kernels. seasonal decomposition mean centering, days similar temperatures opposite sides seasonal cycle—spring versus autumn—exhibit comparable demand profiles. symmetric kernel Ks(x,x′)=K(x,x′)+⋅K(x,−x′)K^s(x, x') = K(x, x') + \cdot K(x, -x') captures reflected structure explicitly: cross-term K(x,−x′)K(x, -x') measures similarity xx reflection x′x', pairs days symmetric seasonal mean assigned high kernel affinity. Models 2 4 psvr family incorporate symmetrisation. Temporal causality requires model selection use rolling-origin cross-validation (Hyndman & Athanasopoulos, 2021) rather random splits. Leaking future observations training fold produce optimistically biased CV estimates invalidate hyperparameter selection step. rolling-origin protocol 10 expanding windows adopted , time series analog multi-seed protocol used companion cross-section case studies. number windows bounded available training horizon: 12-month initial training period 1-month assessment windows 24-month training set, additional folds beyond ten highly correlated contribute diminishing information. total, 12 models evaluated: six psvr variants (Models 1–4, LS-RMSPE appearing twice MAPE- RMSPE-optimised CV selection), three ML baselines (standard RBF-SVR, Random Forest, Linear Regression), three time-series baselines (ARIMAX, ETS, Prophet regressors).

title: Case Study: Victorian Electricity Demand
text: vic_elec dataset tsibbledata records half-hourly electricity demand (MWh) ambient temperature Victoria, Australia 2012–2014. Observations aggregated daily level summing demand, averaging temperature, flagging half-hour marked holiday. Temperature squared appended allow recipe capture well-documented U-shaped demand–temperature relationship without requiring manual interaction terms. Victorian daily electricity demand: dataset summary.  Tip Calendar features (day week, week year, month, quarter, weekend/holiday indicators) generated automatically Date column using step_timeseries_signature() recipe preprocessing pipeline. Temperature^2 included capture nonlinear U-shaped demand–temperature relationship. ETS purely univariate baseline uses separate recipe without features. Daily demand reported GWh. Bayesian optimisation data-driven cost ranges (psvr::cost_psvr_ls_data() LS-SVR; psvr::cost_psvr() ε-SVR) adapts search target scale automatically, manual rescaling needed. strict positivity assumption percentage-error formulation (yk>0y_k > 0) verified .
code: elec_daily <- vic_elec |>   as_tibble() |>   mutate(Date = as_date(Time)) |>   group_by(Date) |>   summarise(     Demand      = sum(Demand) / 1000,  # MWh -> GWh     Temperature = mean(Temperature, na.rm = TRUE),     IsHoliday   = as.integer(any(Holiday)),     .groups     = "drop"   ) |>   mutate(Temperature2 = Temperature^2)  # With Bayesian optimization and psvr::cost_psvr_ls_data() # (data-driven LS-SVR cost range scaling as log2(var(y)*N)), # the search range automatically adapts to the GWh scale of # the target. No manual rescaling needed; ε-SVR box constraints # 100*C/y_k are well-behaved across the relevant cost decade.  stopifnot(all(elec_daily$Demand > 0)) tibble(   Statistic = c(     "n observations", "First date", "Last date",     "Min Demand (MWh)", "Median Demand (MWh)",     "Mean Demand (MWh)", "Max Demand (MWh)"   ),   Value = c(     nrow(elec_daily),     format(min(elec_daily$Date)),     format(max(elec_daily$Date)),     round(min(elec_daily$Demand),    2),     round(median(elec_daily$Demand), 2),     round(mean(elec_daily$Demand),   2),     round(max(elec_daily$Demand),    2)   ) ) |>   knitr::kable(format = "html", col.names = c("Statistic", "Value"),                caption = "Victorian daily electricity demand: dataset summary.") plot_time_series(   elec_daily, Date, Demand,   .interactive = FALSE,   .title = "Victorian daily electricity demand (MWh)" )

title: Case Study: Victorian Electricity Demand
text: Random train/test splits inappropriate time series data allow future observations appear training fold, producing optimistically biased estimates. Rolling-origin CV preserves temporal causality always training past validating future. successive window training set expands one period (cumulative = TRUE), model sees progressively history—mirroring actual deployment conditions. Ten rolling windows used, providing stable estimates generalisation error respecting available training horizon. 24-month training period one-month assessment windows, additional folds beyond ten highly correlated contribute diminishing information.
code: folds <- time_series_cv(   train,   date_var    = Date,   assess      = "1 month",   initial     = "12 months",   slice_limit = 10,   cumulative  = TRUE )  cat("CV windows generated:", nrow(folds), "\n") CV windows generated: 10 folds |>   tk_time_series_cv_plan() |>   plot_time_series_cv_plan(     Date, Demand,     .title       = "Rolling-origin CV plan (10 windows, 1-month horizon)",     .interactive = FALSE   )

title: Case Study: Victorian Electricity Demand
text: preprocessing recipe applies step_timeseries_signature() extract comprehensive set calendar features Date column—including year, quarter, month, week--year, day--week, weekend indicators—without requiring manual mutate() calls. High-cardinality redundant variants (ISO, XTS, POSIX, hour, minute, second, /PM columns) removed via step_rm() avoid multicollinearity reduce dimensionality. remaining nominal predictors (including factor-encoded calendar fields) one-hot encoded step_dummy(), numeric predictors standardised step_normalize() SVR kernel distances dominated scale differences features. ETS, purely univariate method, uses separate recipe Date predictor.
code: # ── Kernel recipe: psvr + SVR-MSE ──────────────────────────── # Continuous features only — RBF kernel degrades with # high-dimensional one-hot encoded categorical features. # DayOfWeek, Month, IsWeekend added manually as numeric. rec_kernel <- recipe(Demand ~ ., data = train) |>   update_role(Date, new_role = "ID") |>   step_mutate(     DayOfWeek = lubridate::wday(Date, week_start = 1),     Month     = lubridate::month(Date),     IsWeekend = as.integer(       lubridate::wday(Date, week_start = 1) >= 6     )   ) |>   step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())  # ── Full recipe: RF + LR ───────────────────────────────────── # Tree-based and linear models benefit from rich calendar # features generated by step_timeseries_signature(). rec_full <- recipe(Demand ~ ., data = train) |>   update_role(Date, new_role = "ID") |>   step_timeseries_signature(Date) |>   step_rm(matches(     "(iso$|xts$|posixct$|hour$|minute$|second$|am.pm$)"   )) |>   step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors(), one_hot = TRUE) |>   step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())  # ── Univariate recipe: TS baselines ────────────────────────── # modeltime engines (auto_arima, ets, prophet) require Date # as a predictor — update_role("ID") hides it from them. rec_univariate <- recipe(Demand ~ Date, data = train)  # Compute rbf_sigma from kernel recipe baked training data train_baked      <- rec_kernel |> prep() |>   bake(new_data = train) rbf_sigma_custom <- rbf_sigma_psvr_data(   train_baked |> select(-Demand, -Date) )  r <- dials::range_get(rbf_sigma_custom, original = FALSE) cat("Kernel recipe predictors:", ncol(train_baked) - 2, "\n") Kernel recipe predictors: 6 cat("Custom rbf_sigma range (log10): [",     round(r$lower, 4), ",", round(r$upper, 4), "]\n") Custom rbf_sigma range (log10): [ -0.524 , 1.476 ]

title: Case Study: Victorian Electricity Demand
text: Model formulations mathematical derivations psvr models, see companion manuscript. LS-RMSPE variants appear twice: MAPE CV (suffix -M) RMSPE CV (suffix -R). model structure identical; CV selection metric differs, handled workflow_map stage. symmetric variants (m2, m4a), symmetry type sym_type ∈ {"even" (= 1), "odd" (= -1)} treated tunable hyperparameter selected jointly cost, svm_margin, rbf_sigma CV. brings electricity grid sizes line cross-section case studies (m2: 96, m4: 32 combinations).
code: # ── psvr models ────────────────────────────────────────────── spec_m1 <- psvr_mape_rbf(   cost = tune(), svm_margin = tune(), rbf_sigma = tune() ) |> set_engine("psvr")  spec_m2 <- psvr_mape_sym_rbf(   cost = tune(), svm_margin = tune(), rbf_sigma = tune(),   sym_type = tune() ) |> set_engine("psvr")  # m3a: LS-RMSPE tuned by MAPE spec_m3a <- psvr_rmspe_rbf(   cost = tune(), rbf_sigma = tune() ) |> set_engine("psvr")  # m4a: LS-RMSPE + Sym. Kernel tuned by MAPE spec_m4a <- psvr_rmspe_sym_rbf(   cost = tune(), rbf_sigma = tune(),   sym_type = tune() ) |> set_engine("psvr")  # ── ML baselines ───────────────────────────────────────────── spec_b1 <- svm_rbf(cost = tune(), rbf_sigma = tune()) |>   set_engine("kernlab") |>   set_mode("regression")  spec_b2 <- rand_forest(   mtry = tune(), trees = 500 ) |>   set_engine("ranger") |>   set_mode("regression")  spec_b4 <- linear_reg() |> set_engine("lm")  # ── TS baselines ───────────────────────────────────────────── spec_ts1 <- arima_reg() |> set_engine("auto_arima")  spec_ts2 <- exp_smoothing() |> set_engine("ets")  spec_ts3 <- prophet_reg(   seasonality_weekly = TRUE,   seasonality_yearly = TRUE ) |> set_engine("prophet")

title: Case Study: Victorian Electricity Demand
code:
# psvr + b1_svrmse: kernel recipe wf_kernel <- workflow_set(   preproc = list(kernel = rec_kernel),   models  = list(     m1        = spec_m1,     m2        = spec_m2,     m3a       = spec_m3a,     m4a       = spec_m4a,     b1_svrmse = spec_b1   ) )  # RF + LR: full recipe with rich calendar features wf_full <- workflow_set(   preproc = list(full = rec_full),   models  = list(     b2_rf = spec_b2,     b4_lm = spec_b4   ) )  # TS baselines: univariate recipe wf_ts <- workflow_set(   preproc = list(univariate = rec_univariate),   models  = list(     ts1_arimax  = spec_ts1,     ts2_ets     = spec_ts2,     ts3_prophet = spec_ts3   ) )  wf_all <- bind_rows(wf_kernel, wf_full, wf_ts)  # ── Per-workflow param_info for Bayesian optimisation ────────── # m1/m2 (ε-SVR):     cost = cost_psvr() static log2 [-2, 10] # m3a/m4a (LS-SVR):  cost = cost_psvr_ls_data(train$Demand) #                    data-driven via var(y)·N heuristic. # rbf_sigma:         data-driven via rbf_sigma_psvr_data() on #                    baked train predictors. # svm_margin:        percentage units [1, 20] for m1/m2. # sym_type for m2/m4a registered automatically from the spec. # b1_svrmse uses the explicit grid_svm via tune_grid; no # param_info needed there. predictor_only   <- train_baked |> select(-Demand, -Date) rbf_sigma_param  <- psvr::rbf_sigma_psvr_data(predictor_only) cost_param_eps   <- psvr::cost_psvr() cost_param_ls    <- psvr::cost_psvr_ls_data(train$Demand) svm_margin_param <- psvr::margin_percentage()  set_pi <- function(wf_set, wf_id, ...) {   pi <- workflowsets::extract_workflow(wf_set, id = wf_id) |>     tune::extract_parameter_set_dials() |>     stats::update(...)   workflowsets::option_add(wf_set, param_info = pi, id = wf_id) }  wf_all <- wf_all |>   set_pi("kernel_m1",          cost       = cost_param_eps,          svm_margin = svm_margin_param,          rbf_sigma  = rbf_sigma_param) |>   set_pi("kernel_m2",          cost       = cost_param_eps,          svm_margin = svm_margin_param,          rbf_sigma  = rbf_sigma_param) |>   set_pi("kernel_m3a",          cost      = cost_param_ls,          rbf_sigma = rbf_sigma_param) |>   set_pi("kernel_m4a",          cost      = cost_param_ls,          rbf_sigma = rbf_sigma_param)  cat("Total workflows:", nrow(wf_all), "
") Total workflows: 10 cat("IDs:
") IDs: print(wf_all$wflow_id) [1] "kernel_m1"              "kernel_m2"              "kernel_m3a"  [4] "kernel_m4a"             "kernel_b1_svrmse"       "full_b2_rf"  [7] "full_b4_lm"             "univariate_ts1_arimax"  "univariate_ts2_ets" [10] "univariate_ts3_prophet" # Explicit hyperparameter grids retained only for non-psvr # baselines (kernlab SVR, random forest). psvr models use # Bayesian optimisation with data-driven param_info. sigma_vals <- c(0.224, 0.707, 2.236, 7.071) C_vals     <- c(0.1, 1, 10, 100)  # b1_svrmse: cost + rbf_sigma (16 combinations) grid_svm <- expand.grid(   cost      = C_vals,   rbf_sigma = sigma_vals,   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # b2_rf: mtry only grid_rf <- data.frame(mtry = c(2L, 3L, 4L))  cat("Grid sizes:
") Grid sizes: cat("  SVR-MSE        (b1):", nrow(grid_svm),          "
") SVR-MSE        (b1): 16 cat("  RF             (b2):", nrow(grid_rf),           "
") RF             (b2): 3

title: Case Study: Victorian Electricity Demand
text: CV metric LS-RMSPE variants m3b m4b use RMSE CV selection criterion—closest native yardstick equivalent RMSPE. m3a m4a use MAPE. model structure identical cases; hyperparameter selection differs.
code: results_file <- here::here(   "vignettes/articles/case-studies/results/electricity-tune-results.rds" )   if (!file.exists(results_file)) {    svm_mse_ids  <- "kernel_b1_svrmse"   rf_ids       <- "full_b2_rf"   ts_ids       <- c("univariate_ts1_arimax",                     "univariate_ts2_ets",                     "univariate_ts3_prophet")   lm_ids       <- "full_b4_lm"    ctrl <- control_grid(     save_pred     = TRUE,     parallel_over = "everything",     verbose       = FALSE   )    ctrl_bayes <- control_bayes(     save_pred  = TRUE,     no_improve = 15L,     verbose    = FALSE,     allow_par  = TRUE,     seed       = 2024L   )    bayes_iters <- list(     kernel_m1  = list(initial = 15L, iter = 35L),     kernel_m2  = list(initial = 15L, iter = 35L),     kernel_m3a = list(initial = 10L, iter = 40L),     kernel_m4a = list(initial = 10L, iter = 40L)   )    metric_set_tune <- metric_set(mape, rmse, rsq)    t0 <- proc.time()   N_WORKERS <- min(12L, parallel::detectCores() - 1L)   if (.Platform$OS.type == "unix") {     future::plan(future::multicore,    workers = N_WORKERS)   } else {     future::plan(future::multisession, workers = N_WORKERS)   }   set.seed(2024)    tune_all <- tryCatch({     # ── psvr models: Bayesian optimisation (GP + EI) ─────────     bayes_results <- list()     for (wf_id in names(bayes_iters)) {       bayes_results[[wf_id]] <- tryCatch({         wf_all |>           filter(wflow_id == wf_id) |>           workflow_map(             fn        = "tune_bayes",             resamples = folds,             initial   = bayes_iters[[wf_id]]$initial,             iter      = bayes_iters[[wf_id]]$iter,             metrics   = metric_set_tune,             control   = ctrl_bayes,             seed      = 2024L,             verbose   = FALSE           )       }, error = function(e) {         message(sprintf("[tuning] %s failed: %s",                         wf_id, conditionMessage(e)))         NULL       })     }      tune_svm_mse <- wf_all |>       filter(wflow_id %in% svm_mse_ids) |>       workflow_map(         fn        = "tune_grid",         resamples = folds,         grid      = grid_svm,         metrics   = metric_set_tune,         control   = ctrl       )      tune_rf <- wf_all |>       filter(wflow_id %in% rf_ids) |>       workflow_map(         fn        = "tune_grid",         resamples = folds,         grid      = grid_rf,         metrics   = metric_set_tune,         control   = ctrl       )      tune_ts <- wf_all |>       filter(wflow_id %in% ts_ids) |>       workflow_map(         fn        = "fit_resamples",         resamples = folds,         metrics   = metric_set_tune,         control   = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE)       )      tune_lm <- wf_all |>       filter(wflow_id %in% lm_ids) |>       workflow_map(         fn        = "fit_resamples",         resamples = folds,         metrics   = metric_set_tune,         control   = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE)       )      elapsed <- round((proc.time() - t0)[["elapsed"]] / 60, 1)     message(sprintf("[tuning] completed in %.1f min", elapsed))     bind_rows(       !!!purrr::compact(bayes_results),       tune_svm_mse, tune_rf,       tune_ts, tune_lm     )    }, error = function(e) {     message("Tuning failed: ", conditionMessage(e))     NULL   }, finally = {     future::plan(future::sequential)   })    if (!is.null(tune_all)) {     saveRDS(tune_all, results_file)   }  } else {   tune_all <- readRDS(results_file) }  cat("Tuning results loaded for",     nrow(tune_all), "workflows.\n") Tuning results loaded for 10 workflows. # Show which workflows have errors   tune_status <- tune_all |>     mutate(       n_results = purrr::map_int(result, function(r) {         tryCatch(nrow(collect_metrics(r)), error = function(e) 0L)       }),       has_error = n_results == 0L     ) |>     select(wflow_id, n_results, has_error)    print(tune_status) # A tibble: 10 × 3    wflow_id               n_results has_error    <chr>                      <int> <lgl>  1 kernel_m1                    150 FALSE  2 kernel_m2                    102 FALSE  3 kernel_m3a                   150 FALSE  4 kernel_m4a                   132 FALSE  5 kernel_b1_svrmse              48 FALSE  6 full_b2_rf                     9 FALSE  7 univariate_ts1_arimax          3 FALSE  8 univariate_ts2_ets             3 FALSE  9 univariate_ts3_prophet         3 FALSE 10 full_b4_lm                     3 FALSE if (any(tune_status$has_error)) {     warning("Some workflows have no results: ",             paste(tune_status$wflow_id[tune_status$has_error],                   collapse = ", "))   }

Kernel",                "SVR-MAPE+SK",   "psvr",   "kernel_m3a",             "kernel_m3a",             "mape",          "m3a",     "LS-RMSPE (MAPE opt.)",                  "LS-RMSPE-M",    "psvr",   "kernel_m3a",             "kernel_m3a",             "rmse",          "m3b",     "LS-RMSPE (RMSPE opt.)",                 "LS-RMSPE-R",    "psvr",   "kernel_m4a",             "kernel_m4a",             "mape",          "m4a",     "LS-RMSPE + Sym. Kernel (MAPE opt.)",    "LS-RMSPE+SK-M", "psvr",   "kernel_m4a",             "kernel_m4a",             "rmse",          "m4b",     "LS-RMSPE + Sym. Kernel (RMSPE opt.)",   "LS-RMSPE+SK-R", "psvr",   "kernel_b1_svrmse",       "kernel_b1_svrmse",       "mape",          "b1",      "ε-SVR (MSE)",                           "SVR-MSE",       "baseline",   "full_b2_rf",             "full_b2_rf",             "mape",          "b2",      "Random Forest",                         "RF",            "baseline",   "full_b4_lm",             "full_b4_lm",             NA_character_,   "b4",      "Linear Regression",                     "LR",            "baseline",   "univariate_ts1_arimax",  "univariate_ts1_arimax",  NA_character_,   "ts1",     "ARIMAX",                                "ARIMAX",        "baseline",   "univariate_ts2_ets",     "univariate_ts2_ets",     NA_character_,   "ts2",     "ETS",                                   "ETS",           "baseline",   "univariate_ts3_prophet", "univariate_ts3_prophet", NA_character_,   "ts3",     "Prophet + regressors",                  "Prophet",       "baseline" )  # ── Step 2: Metric helpers (exact formulas from experiment_helpers.R) ─ mape_fn  <- function(y, yhat) mean(abs((y - yhat) / y)) * 100 rmspe_fn <- function(y, yhat) sqrt(mean(((y - yhat) / y)^2)) * 100 maape_fn <- function(y, yhat) mean(atan(abs((y - yhat) / y))) * (200 / pi) mse_fn   <- function(y, yhat) mean((y - yhat)^2) r2_fn    <- function(y, yhat) 1 - sum((y - yhat)^2) / sum((y - mean(y))^2)  mase_lag7 <- function(actual, predicted, train_actual) {   mae   <- mean(abs(actual - predicted))   denom <- mean(abs(diff(train_actual, lag = 7L)))   mae / denom }  # ── Step 3: Fold actuals lookup ──────────────────────────────────────── # Diagnostic confirmed: collect_predictions() uses zero-padded ids # (Slice01..Slice10) and absolute .row indices in train (not 1-indexed # within each fold). Both must match here for the join to work. fold_actuals <- purrr::imap(folds$splits, function(spl, i) {   asmnt         <- assessment(spl)   asmnt_indices <- rsample::complement(spl)   stopifnot(length(asmnt_indices) == nrow(asmnt))   tibble(     id           = sprintf("Slice%02d", i),     fold_index   = i,     actual       = asmnt$Demand,     .row         = asmnt_indices,     train_demand = list(analysis(spl)$Demand)   ) }) |> bind_rows()  # Verify join keys match before proceeding res_check   <- extract_workflow_set_result(tune_all, "kernel_m1") preds_check <- collect_predictions(res_check) id_mismatch <- !any(unique(preds_check$id) %in% unique(fold_actuals$id)) if (id_mismatch) stop(   "ID format mismatch between predictions and fold_actuals. ",   "Prediction ids: ", paste(unique(preds_check$id)[1:3], collapse = ", "),   " — Fold actuals ids: ", paste(unique(fold_actuals$id)[1:3], collapse = ", ") )  # ── Step 4: Fold-prediction extractor ───────────────────────────────── get_fold_preds <- function(wflow_id, tune_id, select_metric, ...) {   res <- extract_workflow_set_result(tune_all, tune_id)   if (is.na(select_metric)) {     collect_predictions(res)   } else {     best <- select_best(res, metric = select_metric)     collect_predictions(res) |>       filter(.config == best$.config)   } }  # ── Steps 5–6: Per-model per-fold metrics, assemble, save ───────────── results_file <- here::here(   "vignettes/articles/case-studies/results/electricity-results.csv" )   if (!file.exists(results_file)) {    n_folds   <- nrow(folds)   slice_ids <- sprintf("Slice%02d", seq_len(n_folds))    results <- purrr::pmap_dfr(model_registry,     function(wflow_id, tune_id, select_metric,              model_id, label, abbrev, family) {        fold_preds <- tryCatch(         get_fold_preds(wflow_id, tune_id, select_metric),         error = function(e) {           message(sprintf("[export-results] %s failed: %s",                           model_id, conditionMessage(e)))           NULL         }       )        if (is.null(fold_preds)) {         return(tibble(           dataset  = "electricity",           seed     = seq_len(n_folds),           model_id = model_id, label = label,           abbrev   = abbrev,   family = family,           MAPE = NA_real_, RMSPE = NA_real_,           MAAPE = NA_real_, MASE = NA_real_,           MSE  = NA_real_, R2   = NA_real_         ))       }        fold_data <- fold_preds |>         inner_join(fold_actuals, by = c("id", ".row"))        if (nrow(fold_data) == 0L) {         warning(sprintf(           "[export-results] zero rows after join for %s", model_id))         return(tibble(           dataset  = "electricity",           seed     = seq_len(n_folds),           model_id = model_id, label = label,           abbrev   = abbrev,   family = family,           MAPE = NA_real_, RMSPE = NA_real_,           MAAPE = NA_real_, MASE = NA_real_,           MSE  = NA_real_, R2   = NA_real_         ))       }        fold_data |>         group_by(id, fold_index) |>         summarise(           MAPE  = mape_fn(actual,  .pred),           RMSPE = rmspe_fn(actual, .pred),           MAAPE = maape_fn(actual, .pred),           MASE  = mase_lag7(actual, .pred, train_demand[[1]]),           MSE   = mse_fn(actual,   .pred),           R2    = r2_fn(actual,    .pred),           .groups = "drop"         ) |>         mutate(           dataset  = "electricity",           seed     = fold_index,           model_id = model_id, label = label,           abbrev   = abbrev,   family = family         ) |>         select(dataset, seed, model_id, label, abbrev, family,                MAPE, RMSPE, MAAPE, MASE, MSE, R2)     }   )    write_csv(results, results_file)  } else {   results <- read_csv(results_file, show_col_types = FALSE) }  # Verification cat("Rows:", nrow(results), "| Expected: 120\n") Rows: 120 | Expected: 120 cat("NA count:", sum(is.na(results$MAPE)), "\n") NA count: 0 print(results |>   group_by(model_id, abbrev) |>   summarise(mean_MAPE = round(mean(MAPE, na.rm = TRUE), 3),             .groups = "drop") |>   arrange(mean_MAPE)) # A tibble: 12 × 3    model_id abbrev        mean_MAPE    <chr>    <chr>             <dbl>  1 m2       SVR-MAPE+SK        3.13  2 m4a      LS-RMSPE+SK-M      3.17  3 m1       SVR-MAPE           3.18  4 m4b      LS-RMSPE+SK-R      3.18  5 b1       SVR-MSE            3.38  6 m3a      LS-RMSPE-M         3.60  7 m3b      LS-RMSPE-R         3.60  8 b2       RF                 4.65  9 b4       LR                 4.72 10 ts1      ARIMAX             5.43 11 ts2      ETS                6.19 12 ts3      Prophet            6.87

Kernel",            TRUE,   "kernel_m3a",        "mape",         "LS-RMSPE",                          TRUE,   "kernel_m4a",        "mape",         "LS-RMSPE + Sym. Kernel",            TRUE,   "kernel_b1_svrmse",  "mape",         "ε-SVR (MSE)",                       TRUE,   "full_b2_rf",        "mape",         "Random Forest",                     TRUE )  best_hp <- purrr::pmap_dfr(   hp_registry |> dplyr::filter(has_tuning),   function(wflow_id, select_metric, label, has_tuning) {     res  <- extract_workflow_set_result(tune_all, wflow_id)     best <- select_best(res, metric = select_metric)     cv   <- collect_metrics(res) |>       dplyr::filter(.config == best$.config, .metric == "mape") |>       dplyr::slice(1)     tibble::tibble(       Model       = label,       C           = if ("cost"       %in% names(best)) best$cost       else NA_real_,       epsilon     = if ("svm_margin" %in% names(best)) best$svm_margin else NA_real_,       sigma       = if ("rbf_sigma"  %in% names(best)) best$rbf_sigma  else NA_real_,       gamma       = if ("rbf_sigma"  %in% names(best)) 1 / (2 * best$rbf_sigma^2) else NA_real_,       sym_type    = if ("sym_type"   %in% names(best)) as.character(best$sym_type) else NA_character_,       mtry        = if ("mtry"       %in% names(best)) best$mtry       else NA_integer_,       cv_MAPE_pct = cv$mean,       cv_SE       = cv$std_err     )   } )  best_hp |>   dplyr::mutate(     `C`           = ifelse(is.na(C),        "—", format(C)),     `ε`           = ifelse(is.na(epsilon),  "—", format(epsilon)),     `σ`           = ifelse(is.na(sigma),    "—", sprintf("%.3f", sigma)),     `γ`           = ifelse(is.na(gamma),    "—", sprintf("%.3f", gamma)),     `Symmetry`    = ifelse(is.na(sym_type), "—", sym_type),     `mtry`        = ifelse(is.na(mtry),     "—", as.character(mtry)),     `CV MAPE (%)` = sprintf("%.3f ± %.3f", cv_MAPE_pct, cv_SE)   ) |>   dplyr::select(Model, C, `ε`, `σ`, `γ`, Symmetry, mtry, `CV MAPE (%)`) |>   knitr::kable(     format  = "html",     align   = c("l", rep("r", 7)),     caption = paste(       "Hyperparameters selected by 10-fold rolling-origin CV.",       "γ = 1/(2σ²) gives the equivalent RBF gamma in the e1071/sklearn parameterisation.",       "LS-RMSPE and LS-RMSPE + Sym. Kernel select identical (C, σ),",       "isolating the contribution of the symmetric kernel structure."     )   ) |>   kableExtra::kable_styling(     bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),     full_width = FALSE   ) |>   kableExtra::footnote(     general = paste(       "Linear Regression and TS baselines have no tunable hyperparameters",       "in this experiment and are omitted from this table."     ),     general_title = "Note:"   ) hp_results_file <- here::here(   "vignettes/articles/case-studies/results/electricity-best-hp.csv" ) if (!file.exists(hp_results_file)) {   readr::write_csv(best_hp, hp_results_file) }

text: Mean CV MAPE 10 rolling-origin windows. Values shown mean ± standard error. psvr models highlighted blue. Test-set accuracy held-2014 data, sorted MAPE. Reports six metrics CV summary (MAPE, RMSPE, MAAPE %; MASE lag-7 denominator; MSE; R²), matching cross-section case studies. LS-RMSPE (MAPE opt.) LS-RMSPE (RMSPE opt.) share model structure — identical values reflect CV criteria selected hyperparameters dataset. applies LS-RMSPE + Sym. Kernel variants. MASE weekly seasonal naive denominator (lag=7). Values < 1 indicate model outperforms seasonal naive baseline.
code: # Remove workflows with no valid results before ranking   tune_valid <- tune_all |>     filter(purrr::map_lgl(result, function(r) {       tryCatch({         nrow(collect_metrics(r)) > 0       }, error = function(e) FALSE)     }))    rank_results(tune_valid, rank_metric = "mape",                select_best = TRUE) |>   filter(.metric == "mape") |>   mutate(     family = case_when(       str_detect(wflow_id, "^kernel_m")             ~ "psvr",       str_detect(wflow_id, "^(kernel_b|full_b)")    ~ "ML baseline",       str_detect(wflow_id, "^univariate_ts")        ~ "TS baseline"     )   ) |>   ggplot(aes(x = rank, y = mean,              colour = family, label = wflow_id)) +   geom_point(size = 3) +   geom_errorbar(     aes(ymin = mean - 1.96 * std_err,         ymax = mean + 1.96 * std_err),     width = 0.3   ) +   ggrepel::geom_text_repel(size = 3, max.overlaps = 20) +   scale_colour_manual(     values = c("psvr"        = "#2c7bb6",                "ML baseline" = "#d7191c",                "TS baseline" = "#1a9641")   ) +   labs(x      = "Rank",        y      = "CV MAPE (%)",        title  = "Model ranking by CV MAPE (10 rolling windows)",        colour = "Family") +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") options(knitr.kable.NA = "—")  model_labels <- tibble::tribble(   ~wflow_id,                    ~label,   "kernel_m1",                  "SVR-MAPE",   "kernel_m2",                  "SVR-MAPE + Sym. Kernel",   "kernel_m3a",                 "LS-RMSPE (MAPE opt.)",   "kernel_m3b",                 "LS-RMSPE (RMSPE opt.)",   "kernel_m4a",                 "LS-RMSPE + Sym. Kernel (MAPE opt.)",   "kernel_m4b",                 "LS-RMSPE + Sym. Kernel (RMSPE opt.)",   "kernel_b1_svrmse",           "ε-SVR (MSE)",   "full_b2_rf",                 "Random Forest",   "full_b4_lm",                 "Linear Regression",   "univariate_ts1_arimax",      "ARIMAX",   "univariate_ts2_ets",         "ETS",   "univariate_ts3_prophet",     "Prophet + regressors" )  family_labels <- tibble::tribble(   ~wflow_id,                    ~family,   "kernel_m1",                  "psvr",   "kernel_m2",                  "psvr",   "kernel_m3a",                 "psvr",   "kernel_m3b",                 "psvr",   "kernel_m4a",                 "psvr",   "kernel_m4b",                 "psvr",   "kernel_b1_svrmse",           "ML baseline",   "full_b2_rf",                 "ML baseline",   "full_b4_lm",                 "ML baseline",   "univariate_ts1_arimax",      "TS baseline",   "univariate_ts2_ets",         "TS baseline",   "univariate_ts3_prophet",     "TS baseline" )  # tune_all only has 10 workflows (m3b/m4b share with m3a/m4a) # Use tune_valid for CV table (excludes failed workflows) cv_labels <- model_labels |>   filter(!wflow_id %in% c("kernel_m3b", "kernel_m4b")) cv_families <- family_labels |>   filter(!wflow_id %in% c("kernel_m3b", "kernel_m4b"))  cv_summary <- collect_metrics(tune_valid) |>   filter(.metric == "mape") |>   group_by(wflow_id) |>   slice_min(mean, n = 1) |>   ungroup() |>   left_join(cv_labels,    by = "wflow_id") |>   left_join(cv_families,  by = "wflow_id") |>   arrange(mean)  cv_summary |>   mutate(     `CV MAPE (%)` = sprintf("%.3f ± %.3f",                             mean, std_err)   ) |>   select(Model = label, Family = family,          `CV MAPE (%)`) |>   knitr::kable(     format  = "html",     escape  = FALSE,     caption = paste(       "Mean CV MAPE over 10 rolling-origin windows.",       "Values shown as mean ± standard error.",       "psvr models highlighted in blue."     )   ) |>   kableExtra::kable_styling(     bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),     full_width = FALSE   ) |>   kableExtra::row_spec(     which(cv_summary$family == "psvr"),     background = "#eaf4fb"   ) # Helper: finalize workflow and refit on full train set finalize_and_fit <- function(wflow_id_str,                               metric = "mape") {   wf  <- extract_workflow(wf_all, id = wflow_id_str)   res <- extract_workflow_set_result(tune_all,                                       wflow_id_str)   # fit_resamples results have no tunable params —   # use workflow directly without select_best   has_params <- tryCatch({     best <- select_best(res, metric = metric)     TRUE   }, error = function(e) FALSE)    if (has_params) {     best <- select_best(res, metric = metric)     wf   <- finalize_workflow(wf, best)   }   tryCatch(     last_fit(wf, splits,              metrics = metric_set(mape, rmse, rsq)),     error = function(e) {       warning(sprintf(         "last_fit failed for %s: %s",         wflow_id_str, conditionMessage(e)))       NULL     }   ) }  # No-tune models: fit directly fit_b4 <- extract_workflow(wf_all, "full_b4_lm") |>   last_fit(splits, metrics = metric_set(mape, rmse, rsq))  fit_ets <- extract_workflow(wf_all,                             "univariate_ts2_ets") |>   last_fit(splits, metrics = metric_set(mape, rmse, rsq))  fits <- list(   kernel_m1              = finalize_and_fit("kernel_m1",                                              "mape"),   kernel_m2              = finalize_and_fit("kernel_m2",                                              "mape"),   kernel_m3a             = finalize_and_fit("kernel_m3a",                                              "mape"),   kernel_m3b             = finalize_and_fit("kernel_m3a",                                              "rmse"),   kernel_m4a             = finalize_and_fit("kernel_m4a",                                              "mape"),   kernel_m4b             = finalize_and_fit("kernel_m4a",                                              "rmse"),   kernel_b1_svrmse       = finalize_and_fit(                              "kernel_b1_svrmse", "mape"),   full_b2_rf             = finalize_and_fit("full_b2_rf",                                              "mape"),   full_b4_lm             = fit_b4,   univariate_ts1_arimax  = finalize_and_fit(                              "univariate_ts1_arimax",                              "mape"),   univariate_ts2_ets     = fit_ets,   univariate_ts3_prophet = finalize_and_fit(                              "univariate_ts3_prophet",                              "mape") ) # Compute the same six metrics used in CV reporting # (Section 9.1) on the held-out 2014 test set. y_train_full <- train$Demand  test_metrics <- purrr::imap(fits, function(fit, id) {   if (is.null(fit)) return(NULL)   preds <- tryCatch(collect_predictions(fit),                     error = function(e) NULL)   if (is.null(preds) || nrow(preds) == 0L) return(NULL)   yhat <- preds$.pred   yact <- preds$Demand   tibble(     wflow_id = id,     MAPE  = mape_fn(yact,  yhat),     RMSPE = rmspe_fn(yact, yhat),     MAAPE = maape_fn(yact, yhat),     MASE  = mase_lag7(yact, yhat, y_train_full),     MSE   = mse_fn(yact,   yhat),     R2    = r2_fn(yact,    yhat)   ) }) |>   purrr::compact() |>   dplyr::bind_rows() |>   left_join(model_labels,  by = "wflow_id") |>   left_join(family_labels, by = "wflow_id") |>   arrange(MAPE)  test_metrics |>   select(Model = label, Family = family,          `MAPE (%)`  = MAPE,          `RMSPE (%)` = RMSPE,          `MAAPE (%)` = MAAPE,          `MASE`      = MASE,          `MSE`       = MSE,          `R²`        = R2) |>   mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(.x, 4))) |>   knitr::kable(     format  = "html",     escape  = FALSE,     caption = paste(       "Test-set accuracy on held-out 2014 data, sorted by MAPE.",       "Reports the same six metrics as the CV summary",       "(MAPE, RMSPE, MAAPE in %; MASE with lag-7 denominator;",       "MSE; R²), matching the cross-section case studies.",       "LS-RMSPE (MAPE opt.) and LS-RMSPE (RMSPE opt.) share the",       "same model structure — identical values reflect that both",       "CV criteria selected the same hyperparameters on this dataset.",       "Same applies to LS-RMSPE + Sym. Kernel variants."     )   ) |>   kableExtra::kable_styling(     bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),     full_width = FALSE   ) |>   kableExtra::row_spec(     which(test_metrics$family == "psvr"),     background = "#eaf4fb"   ) # For electricity demand, MASE uses lag-7 (weekly seasonal # naive) as denominator — standard for daily data with # weekly seasonality (Hyndman & Koehler, 2006) mase_lag7 <- function(actual, predicted, train_actual) {   mae   <- mean(abs(actual - predicted))   denom <- mean(abs(diff(train_actual, lag = 7L)))   mae / denom }  y_train <- train$Demand y_test  <- test$Demand  mase_results <- purrr::imap(fits, function(fit, id) {   if (is.null(fit)) return(NULL)   preds <- tryCatch(     collect_predictions(fit)$.pred,     error = function(e) NULL   )   if (is.null(preds)) return(NULL)   tibble(     wflow_id  = id,     MASE_lag7 = mase_lag7(y_test, preds, y_train)   ) }) |>   purrr::compact() |>   dplyr::bind_rows() |>   left_join(model_labels, by = "wflow_id") |>   arrange(MASE_lag7)  mase_results |>   select(Model = label, `MASE (lag-7)` = MASE_lag7) |>   mutate(`MASE (lag-7)` = round(`MASE (lag-7)`, 4)) |>   knitr::kable(     format  = "html",     caption = paste(       "MASE with weekly seasonal naive denominator (lag=7).",       "Values < 1 indicate the model outperforms",       "the seasonal naive baseline."     )   ) |>   kableExtra::kable_styling(     bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),     full_width = FALSE   ) # Combine all test predictions for plotting all_preds <- purrr::imap(fits, function(fit, id) {   if (is.null(fit)) return(NULL)   tryCatch({     preds <- augment(fit)     # augment() returns the test data with .pred column attached     # to the correct rows automatically     preds |>       mutate(wflow_id = id) |>       select(Date, .pred, wflow_id)   }, error = function(e) {     message(sprintf("[forecast-plot] %s failed: %s",                     id, conditionMessage(e)))     NULL   }) }) |>   purrr::compact() |>   dplyr::bind_rows() |>   left_join(model_labels, by = "wflow_id")  # Sanity check: the plot must have predictions stopifnot(   "all_preds is empty"         = nrow(all_preds) > 0L,   "all .pred values are NA"    = !all(is.na(all_preds$.pred)),   "no Date column"             = "Date" %in% names(all_preds) )  # Plot actual vs predicted for all models ggplot() +   geom_line(     data = test,     aes(x = Date, y = Demand),     colour = "black", linewidth = 0.8, alpha = 0.7   ) +   geom_line(     data = all_preds,     aes(x = Date, y = .pred,         colour = label),     linewidth = 0.5, alpha = 0.7   ) +   scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "turbo") +   labs(     x      = NULL,     y      = "Daily Demand (MWh)",     title  = sprintf(       "Test-set forecasts: %d models vs. actual (2014)",       length(unique(all_preds$wflow_id))     ),     colour = "Model"   ) +   theme(legend.position = "bottom",         legend.text     = element_text(size = 8)) +   guides(colour = guide_legend(nrow = 3))

code: options(knitr.kable.NA = "—")  model_labels <- tibble::tribble(   ~wflow_id,                    ~label,   "kernel_m1",                  "SVR-MAPE",   "kernel_m2",                  "SVR-MAPE + Sym. Kernel",   "kernel_m3a",                 "LS-RMSPE (MAPE opt.)",   "kernel_m3b",                 "LS-RMSPE (RMSPE opt.)",   "kernel_m4a",                 "LS-RMSPE + Sym. tune_all only has 10 workflows (m3b/m4b share with m3a/m4a) # Use tune_valid for CV table (excludes failed workflows) cv_labels <- model_labels |>   filter(!wflow_id %in% c("kernel_m3b", "kernel_m4b")) cv_families <- family_labels |>   filter(!wflow_id %in% c("kernel_m3b", "kernel_m4b"))  cv_summary <- collect_metrics(tune_valid) |>   filter(.metric == "mape") |>   group_by(wflow_id) |>   slice_min(mean, n = 1) |>   ungroup() |>   left_join(cv_labels,    by = "wflow_id") |>   left_join(cv_families,  by = "wflow_id") |>   arrange(mean)  cv_summary |>   mutate(     `CV MAPE (%)` = sprintf("%.3f ± %.3f",                             mean, std_err)   ) |>   select(Model = label, Family = family,          `CV MAPE (%)`) |>   knitr::kable(     format  = "html",     escape  = FALSE,     caption = paste(       "Mean CV MAPE over 10 rolling-origin windows.",       "Values shown as mean ± standard error.",       "psvr models highlighted in blue."     )   ) |>   kableExtra::kable_styling(     bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),     full_width = FALSE   ) |>   kableExtra::row_spec(     which(cv_summary$family == "psvr"),     background = "#eaf4fb"   )

code: # Helper: finalize workflow and refit on full train set finalize_and_fit <- function(wflow_id_str,                               metric = "mape") {   wf  <- extract_workflow(wf_all, id = wflow_id_str)   res <- extract_workflow_set_result(tune_all,                                       wflow_id_str)   # fit_resamples results have no tunable params —   # use workflow directly without select_best   has_params <- tryCatch({     best <- select_best(res, metric = metric)     TRUE   }, error = function(e) FALSE)    if (has_params) {     best <- select_best(res, metric = metric)     wf   <- finalize_workflow(wf, best)   }   tryCatch(     last_fit(wf, splits,              metrics = metric_set(mape, rmse, rsq)),     error = function(e) {       warning(sprintf(         "last_fit failed for %s: %s",         wflow_id_str, conditionMessage(e)))       NULL     }   ) }  # No-tune models: fit directly fit_b4 <- extract_workflow(wf_all, "full_b4_lm") |>   last_fit(splits, metrics = metric_set(mape, rmse, rsq))  fit_ets <- extract_workflow(wf_all,                             "univariate_ts2_ets") |>   last_fit(splits, metrics = metric_set(mape, rmse, rsq))  fits <- list(   kernel_m1              = finalize_and_fit("kernel_m1",                                              "mape"),   kernel_m2              = finalize_and_fit("kernel_m2",                                              "mape"),   kernel_m3a             = finalize_and_fit("kernel_m3a",                                              "mape"),   kernel_m3b             = finalize_and_fit("kernel_m3a",                                              "rmse"),   kernel_m4a             = finalize_and_fit("kernel_m4a",                                              "mape"),   kernel_m4b             = finalize_and_fit("kernel_m4a",                                              "rmse"),   kernel_b1_svrmse       = finalize_and_fit(                              "kernel_b1_svrmse", "mape"),   full_b2_rf             = finalize_and_fit("full_b2_rf",                                              "mape"),   full_b4_lm             = fit_b4,   univariate_ts1_arimax  = finalize_and_fit(                              "univariate_ts1_arimax",                              "mape"),   univariate_ts2_ets     = fit_ets,   univariate_ts3_prophet = finalize_and_fit(                              "univariate_ts3_prophet",                              "mape") )

text: MASE weekly seasonal naive denominator (lag=7). Values < 1 indicate model outperforms seasonal naive baseline.
code: # For electricity demand, MASE uses lag-7 (weekly seasonal # naive) as denominator — standard for daily data with # weekly seasonality (Hyndman & Koehler, 2006) mase_lag7 <- function(actual, predicted, train_actual) {   mae   <- mean(abs(actual - predicted))   denom <- mean(abs(diff(train_actual, lag = 7L)))   mae / denom }  y_train <- train$Demand y_test  <- test$Demand  mase_results <- purrr::imap(fits, function(fit, id) {   if (is.null(fit)) return(NULL)   preds <- tryCatch(     collect_predictions(fit)$.pred,     error = function(e) NULL   )   if (is.null(preds)) return(NULL)   tibble(     wflow_id  = id,     MASE_lag7 = mase_lag7(y_test, preds, y_train)   ) }) |>   purrr::compact() |>   dplyr::bind_rows() |>   left_join(model_labels, by = "wflow_id") |>   arrange(MASE_lag7)  mase_results |>   select(Model = label, `MASE (lag-7)` = MASE_lag7) |>   mutate(`MASE (lag-7)` = round(`MASE (lag-7)`, 4)) |>   knitr::kable(     format  = "html",     caption = paste(       "MASE with weekly seasonal naive denominator (lag=7).",       "Values < 1 indicate the model outperforms",       "the seasonal naive baseline."     )   ) |>   kableExtra::kable_styling(     bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),     full_width = FALSE   )

code: # Combine all test predictions for plotting all_preds <- purrr::imap(fits, function(fit, id) {   if (is.null(fit)) return(NULL)   tryCatch({     preds <- augment(fit)     # augment() returns the test data with .pred column attached     # to the correct rows automatically     preds |>       mutate(wflow_id = id) |>       select(Date, .pred, wflow_id)   }, error = function(e) {     message(sprintf("[forecast-plot] %s failed: %s",                     id, conditionMessage(e)))     NULL   }) }) |>   purrr::compact() |>   dplyr::bind_rows() |>   left_join(model_labels, by = "wflow_id")  # Sanity check: the plot must have predictions stopifnot(   "all_preds is empty"         = nrow(all_preds) > 0L,   "all .pred values are NA"    = !all(is.na(all_preds$.pred)),   "no Date column"             = "Date" %in% names(all_preds) )  # Plot actual vs predicted for all models ggplot() +   geom_line(     data = test,     aes(x = Date, y = Demand),     colour = "black", linewidth = 0.8, alpha = 0.7   ) +   geom_line(     data = all_preds,     aes(x = Date, y = .pred,         colour = label),     linewidth = 0.5, alpha = 0.7   ) +   scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "turbo") +   labs(     x      = NULL,     y      = "Daily Demand (MWh)",     title  = sprintf(       "Test-set forecasts: %d models vs. actual (2014)",       length(unique(all_preds$wflow_id))     ),     colour = "Model"   ) +   theme(legend.position = "bottom",         legend.text     = element_text(size = 8)) +   guides(colour = guide_legend(nrow = 3))

text: benchmark follows 30-seed protocol: seed 1–30, 768 observations randomly split 80% training (≈ 614 rows) 20% test (≈ 154 rows). Hyperparameters selected via 5-fold cross-validation training set, minimizing model’s native CV metric. Grid sizes 48 combinations SVR-MAPE Log-SVR; 96 SVR-MAPE+SK (symmetry type ∈ {-1, 1} jointly tuned cost, margin, bandwidth); 16 LS-RMSPE variants ε-SVR (MSE); 32 LS-RMSPE+SK (jointly tuned cost bandwidth); 6 Random Forest; 8 XGBoost; tuning Linear Regression, WLS, Quantile Regression. Six metrics recorded held-test set: MAPE, RMSPE, MAAPE, MASE, MSE, R². Prior fitting, predictor features standardized zero mean unit variance using statistics computed training fold ; test-set features scaled parameters prevent data leakage. MASE uses lag-1 naive denominator; Energy Efficiency data temporal ordering, denominator depends row order within split interpreted accordingly. Quantile regression (b7a) fitted dataset due near-singular design matrix caused ordinal features X6 X8; results b7a reported NA across seeds.

text: Classical SVR minimises absolute-error losses (MAE, MSE), misaligned scale-free accuracy criteria standard forecasting. error 1 unit negligible target 1 000 large 2. psvr implements four SVR variants derived percentage-error loss functions (Benavides-Herrera et al., 2026): models require strictly positive targets (y > 0), condition percentage residuals well-defined.

text: reproduce experiments reported Tables 1–2 paper using Boston Housing dataset (Harrison & Rubinfeld, 1978). 506 observations strictly positive median home values (MEDV, thousands USD), making MAPE well-defined throughout.
code: library(psvr) library(ggplot2)  # Boston Housing is available in the MASS package data("Boston", package = "MASS")  # Target: median home value (all > 0) y_all <- Boston$medv X_raw <- as.matrix(Boston[, setdiff(names(Boston), "medv")])  stopifnot(all(y_all > 0)) cat("N =", nrow(X_raw), "  p =", ncol(X_raw),     "  y range: [", round(min(y_all), 1), ",", round(max(y_all), 1), "]\n") #> N = 506   p = 13   y range: [ 5 , 50 ]

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: Features standardised using training-set statistics RBF kernel operates comparable scale across 13 predictors.
code: set.seed(42) n      <- nrow(X_raw) tr_idx <- sample(n, floor(0.7 * n))  X_raw_tr <- X_raw[tr_idx, ];  y_tr <- y_all[tr_idx] X_raw_te <- X_raw[-tr_idx, ]; y_te <- y_all[-tr_idx]  # Standardise: centre and scale by training mean/sd col_mean <- colMeans(X_raw_tr) col_sd   <- apply(X_raw_tr, 2, sd) X_tr <- scale(X_raw_tr, center = col_mean, scale = col_sd) X_te <- scale(X_raw_te, center = col_mean, scale = col_sd)

title: Getting Started with psvr
code: mape  <- function(y, yhat) mean(abs(y - yhat) / y) * 100 rmspe <- function(y, yhat) sqrt(mean(((y - yhat) / y)^2)) * 100 r2    <- function(y, yhat) 1 - sum((y - yhat)^2) / sum((y - mean(y))^2)

code: lm_df_tr <- as.data.frame(X_tr) lm_df_te <- as.data.frame(X_te) lm_fit   <- lm(y_tr ~ ., data = lm_df_tr) lm_pred  <- predict(lm_fit, newdata = lm_df_te)  cat(sprintf("Linear regression — MAPE: %.2f%%  RMSPE: %.2f%%  R²: %.4f\n",             mape(y_te, lm_pred), rmspe(y_te, lm_pred), r2(y_te, lm_pred))) #> Linear regression — MAPE: 16.29%  RMSPE: 22.48%  R²: 0.7730

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: LS-SVR formulation replaces QP linear system using quadratic penalty percentage residuals. dual reduces : [0𝟏⊤𝟏Ω+YΓ][b𝛂]=[0𝐲] \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \mathbf{1}^\top \\ \mathbf{1} & \Omega + Y_\Gamma \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} b \\ \boldsymbol{\alpha} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \mathbf{y} \end{bmatrix} YΓ=diag(y12/Γ,…,yN2/Γ)Y_\Gamma = \operatorname{diag}(y_1^2/\Gamma, \ldots, y_N^2/\Gamma).
code: # make_kernel() returns a closure K(xi, xj) = exp(-||xi - xj||^2 / (2 sigma^2)) K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1)  # gamma = 5000: regularisation; larger gamma -> smaller Y_Gamma diagonal -> tighter fit fit_ls <- rmspe_lssvr(X_tr, y_tr, kernel = K, gamma = 5000) pred_ls <- predict(fit_ls, X_te)  cat(sprintf("LS-SVR RMSPE  — MAPE: %.2f%%  RMSPE: %.2f%%  R²: %.4f\n",             mape(y_te, pred_ls), rmspe(y_te, pred_ls), r2(y_te, pred_ls))) #> LS-SVR RMSPE  — MAPE: 15.46%  RMSPE: 27.21%  R²: 0.7277 print(fit_ls) #>  #> LS-SVR with RMSPE loss  [psvr_rmspe] #>  #>   Kernel:        RBF (sigma = 1) #>   Gamma:         5000 #>   Training obs.: 354 cf_ls <- coef(fit_ls) # alpha: N dual variables; weight each training point's kernel contribution #        in f(x) = sum_k alpha_k K(x_k, x) + b (all N points, no sparsity) # b:     bias / intercept term # X_sv:  all N training inputs stored for prediction cat(sprintf("b = %.4f  |  alpha range: [%.4f, %.4f]\n",             cf_ls$b, min(cf_ls$alpha), max(cf_ls$alpha))) #> b = 22.5268  |  alpha range: [-59.7517, 43.7321]

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: ε-SVR formulation optimises QP sample-dependent box constraints |βk|≤100C/yk|\beta_k| \le 100C/y_k: tighter bounds small targets, concentrating model capacity low-magnitude observations.
code:
# C = 10: per-sample box bound |beta_k| <= 100*C/y_k; eps = 1: tube width (% of y_k) fit_ep <- mape_svr(X_tr, y_tr, kernel = K, C = 10, eps = 1) pred_ep <- predict(fit_ep, X_te)  cat(sprintf("ε-SVR MAPE    — MAPE: %.2f%%  RMSPE: %.2f%%  R²: %.4f
",             mape(y_te, pred_ep), rmspe(y_te, pred_ep), r2(y_te, pred_ep))) #> ε-SVR MAPE    — MAPE: 15.76%  RMSPE: 27.54%  R²: 0.7617 cat(sprintf("Support vectors: %d / %d
", length(fit_ep$beta), nrow(X_tr))) #> Support vectors: 327 / 354 print(fit_ep) #>  #> Epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss  [psvr_mape] #>  #>   Kernel:          RBF (sigma = 1) #>   C:               10 #>   eps:             1 #>   Training obs.:   354 #>   Support vectors: 327 (92.4%) cf_ep <- coef(fit_ep) # alpha: beta_k = alpha_k - alpha_k* for each support vector; non-zero only for #        training points outside the percentage-error ε-tube (sparse) # b:     bias / intercept term # X_sv:  training rows corresponding to support vectors only cat(sprintf("b = %.4f  |  alpha range: [%.4f, %.4f]
",             cf_ep$b, min(cf_ep$alpha), max(cf_ep$alpha))) #> b = 23.1087  |  alpha range: [-110.6876, 82.6446]

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: Test-set performance Boston Housing (70/30 split, RBF kernel, single run). psvr models improve linear baseline respective percentage-error objectives. LS-SVR formulation (Model 3) minimises RMSPE directly achieves lowest squared-percentage error; ε-SVR formulation (Model 1) targets MAPE sample-dependent box constraints dual variables. results consistent Tables 1–2 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026), SVR-MAPE reduces test-set MAPE 15.23% (classical ε-SVR) around 10% relative linear baseline 17.85%.
code: results <- data.frame(   Model = c("Linear regression", "LS-SVR RMSPE (Model 3)",             "\u03b5-SVR MAPE (Model 1)"),   MAPE  = c(mape(y_te,  lm_pred),             mape(y_te,  pred_ls),             mape(y_te,  pred_ep)),   RMSPE = c(rmspe(y_te, lm_pred),             rmspe(y_te, pred_ls),             rmspe(y_te, pred_ep)),   R2    = c(r2(y_te,    lm_pred),             r2(y_te,    pred_ls),             r2(y_te,    pred_ep)) ) results[, 2:4] <- round(results[, 2:4], 2) knitr::kable(results, col.names = c("Model", "MAPE (%)", "RMSPE (%)", "R²"),              align = "lrrr",              caption = paste("Test-set performance on Boston Housing",                              "(70/30 split, RBF kernel, single run)."))

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: vignette verifies R implementations psvr reproduce numerical results reported Tables 1–2 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026), originally computed Python using cvxopt (Models 1 & 2) numpy.linalg.lstsq (Models 3 & 4). verification strategy : Use dataset, split seed, hyperparameters Python experiments. Report four metrics: MAPE, RMSPE, R², MSE test set. Compare R outputs paper reference values. Small numerical differences expected acceptable: Python implementation uses cvxopt QP (Models 1 & 2) pseudoinverse via numpy.linalg.lstsq linear system (Models 3 & 4), whereas psvr uses osqp base::solve(), respectively. approaches solve mathematical problem use different numerical algorithms different stopping tolerances, can produce discrepancies order 0.01–0.1 percentage points reported metrics.

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
code: library(psvr) library(ggplot2)  # Boston Housing — all 506 observations, MEDV strictly positive data("Boston", package = "MASS")  y_all <- Boston$medv X_raw <- as.matrix(Boston[, setdiff(names(Boston), "medv")])  stopifnot(all(y_all > 0)) cat("N =", nrow(X_raw), " p =", ncol(X_raw),     " y range: [", round(min(y_all), 1), ",", round(max(y_all), 1), "]\n") #> N = 506  p = 13  y range: [ 5 , 50 ]

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: Python experiments used train_test_split(..., test_size=0.30, random_state=4). replicate set.seed(4) stratified-equivalent random draw.
code: set.seed(4) n      <- nrow(X_raw) tr_idx <- sample(n, floor(0.7 * n))  X_raw_tr <- X_raw[tr_idx, ];  y_tr <- y_all[tr_idx] X_raw_te <- X_raw[-tr_idx, ]; y_te <- y_all[-tr_idx]  cat("Training:", nrow(X_raw_tr), " Test:", nrow(X_raw_te), "\n") #> Training: 354  Test: 152

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: Features centred scaled using training-set mean standard deviation, mimicking StandardScaler().fit(X_train).transform(...).
code: col_mean <- colMeans(X_raw_tr) col_sd   <- apply(X_raw_tr, 2, sd)  X_tr <- scale(X_raw_tr, center = col_mean, scale = col_sd) X_te <- scale(X_raw_te, center = col_mean, scale = col_sd)

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
code: mape_fn  <- function(y, yhat) mean(abs(y - yhat) / y) * 100 rmspe_fn <- function(y, yhat) sqrt(mean(((y - yhat) / y)^2)) * 100 r2_fn    <- function(y, yhat) 1 - sum((y - yhat)^2) / sum((y - mean(y))^2) mse_fn   <- function(y, yhat) mean((y - yhat)^2)

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: Hyperparameters Table 1 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026): Python RBF kernel uses gamma_py convention K(𝐱i,𝐱j)=exp(−γpy∥𝐱i−𝐱j∥2)K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\gamma_{\text{py}} \|\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\|^2), equivalent psvr RBF convention K(𝐱i,𝐱j)=exp(−∥𝐱i−𝐱j∥2/2σ2)K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\|\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\|^2 / 2\sigma^2) σ=1/(2γpy)\sigma = \sqrt{1 / (2\,\gamma_{\text{py}})}.
code:
sigma_m3 <- sqrt(1 / (2 * 0.050967)) cat("Model 3 — sigma:", round(sigma_m3, 6), "
") #> Model 3 — sigma: 3.132135  K3  <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = sigma_m3) fit_m3 <- rmspe_lssvr(X_tr, y_tr, kernel = K3, gamma = 16962.540770) pred_m3 <- predict(fit_m3, X_te)  m3_mape  <- mape_fn(y_te, pred_m3) m3_rmspe <- rmspe_fn(y_te, pred_m3) m3_r2    <- r2_fn(y_te, pred_m3) m3_mse   <- mse_fn(y_te, pred_m3)  cat(sprintf(   "Model 3 (R) — MAPE: %.4f%%  RMSPE: %.4f%%  R²: %.6f  MSE: %.6f
",   m3_mape, m3_rmspe, m3_r2, m3_mse )) #> Model 3 (R) — MAPE: 11.0495%  RMSPE: 18.7205%  R²: 0.819584  MSE: 14.127632 print(fit_m3) #>  #> LS-SVR with RMSPE loss  [psvr_rmspe] #>  #>   Kernel:        RBF (sigma = 3.13213) #>   Gamma:         16962.5 #>   Training obs.: 354 cf_m3 <- coef(fit_m3) # alpha: N dual variables; weight each training point in f(x) = sum_k alpha_k K(x_k, x) + b # b:     bias / intercept term # X_sv:  all N training inputs (LS-SVR — no sparsity, every point contributes) cat(sprintf("b = %.4f  |  alpha range: [%.4f, %.4f]
",             cf_m3$b, min(cf_m3$alpha), max(cf_m3$alpha))) #> b = 25.1360  |  alpha range: [-774.4447, 307.9511]

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: table juxtaposes R results computed Python reference values reported Tables 1–2 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026). metrics directly comparable two implementations use different train/test splits: set.seed(4); sample() R seeds R’s Mersenne Twister, random_state=4 sklearn seeds numpy’s Mersenne Twister. two generators produce different random sequences integer seed, 152 test observations differ two runs. mathematical equivalence implementations verified KKT unit tests tests/testthat/, matching held-metrics. Test-set metrics — Boston Housing, 70/30 split. R rows: set.seed(4) R’s RNG. Python rows: random_state=4 numpy’s RNG (different test set). Splits equivalent; metric differences reflect different test observations, implementation errors. NA: reported paper.
code: # Python reference values from Tables 1-2 of Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026) py_m3_mape  <- 10.06        # Table 1, Model 3 — MAPE (%) py_m3_rmspe <- NA_real_     # Table 1, Model 3 — RMSPE (%) not reported py_m3_r2    <- 0.8959       # Table 1, Model 3 — R² py_m3_mse   <- NA_real_     # Table 1, Model 3 — MSE not reported  py_m1_mape  <- 10.29        # Table 2, Model 1 — MAPE (%) py_m1_rmspe <- NA_real_     # Table 2, Model 1 — RMSPE (%) not reported py_m1_r2    <- 0.8602       # Table 2, Model 1 — R² py_m1_mse   <- NA_real_     # Table 2, Model 1 — MSE not reported  cmp <- data.frame(   Model      = c("Model 3 — LS-SVR RMSPE (R)",                  "Model 3 — LS-SVR RMSPE (Python, Table 1)",                  "Model 1 — \u03b5-SVR MAPE (R)",                  "Model 1 — \u03b5-SVR MAPE (Python, Table 2)"),   `MAPE (%)`  = round(c(m3_mape,  py_m3_mape,  m1_mape,  py_m1_mape),  4),   `RMSPE (%)` = round(c(m3_rmspe, py_m3_rmspe, m1_rmspe, py_m1_rmspe), 4),   `R2`        = round(c(m3_r2,    py_m3_r2,    m1_r2,    py_m1_r2),    6),   `MSE`       = round(c(m3_mse,   py_m3_mse,   m1_mse,   py_m1_mse),   6),   check.names = FALSE )  knitr::kable(   cmp,   col.names = c("Implementation", "MAPE (%)", "RMSPE (%)", "R\u00b2", "MSE"),   align     = "lrrrr",   caption   = paste(     "Test-set metrics — Boston Housing, 70/30 split.",     "R rows: set.seed(4) with R's RNG.",     "Python rows: random_state=4 with numpy's RNG (different test set).",     "Splits are not equivalent; metric differences reflect different test",     "observations, not implementation errors.",     "NA: not reported in the paper."   ) )

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: observed gaps (e.g. R² 0.82 vs 0.90 Model 3) evidence implementation bug. Numerical investigation confirms three things: Non-equivalent splits dominant cause. set.seed(4) seeds R’s Mersenne Twister; random_state=4 seeds numpy’s Mersenne Twister. independent generators happen share API convention seed integer produce entirely different random sequences. 152 test observations selected different, metrics computed different data. linear solver factor. Replacing base::solve() MASS::ginv() (pseudoinverse via SVD, matching Python’s numpy.linalg.lstsq) changes predictions 3 × 10⁻¹² dataset. augmented system condition number κ ≈ 2 100, LU decomposition SVD agree machine precision. QP solver minor factor. osqp (ADMM) cvxopt (interior-point) solve QP different tolerances, producing slightly different dual variables. accounts 0.1–0.2 percentage points MAPE, multi-point gaps seen . correct way verify mathematical equivalence KKT conditions: unit tests tests/testthat/ check fitted models satisfy KKT stationarity, complementary slackness, feasibility conditions within solver tolerance, independently particular split.

title: tidymodels workflow with psvr
text: synthetic even-function dataset package README: y=2+x12+0.5x22+εy = 2 + x_1^2 + 0.5\,x_2^2 + \varepsilon, ε∼𝒩(0,0.12)\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\,0.1^2). Targets strictly positive construction (y>0y > 0).
code: set.seed(42) n   <- 200 x1  <- runif(n, -3, 3) x2  <- runif(n, -3, 3) y   <- 2 + x1^2 + 0.5 * x2^2 + rnorm(n, sd = 0.1) dat <- data.frame(y = y, x1 = x1, x2 = x2)

title: tidymodels workflow with psvr
code: set.seed(1) split <- initial_split(dat, prop = 0.75) train <- training(split) test  <- testing(split)

title: tidymodels workflow with psvr
text: Centre scale predictors RBF kernel operates standardised feature space.
code: rec <- recipe(y ~ x1 + x2, data = train) |>   step_normalize(all_predictors())

title: tidymodels workflow with psvr
text: cost (maps Γ\Gamma) rbf_sigma (RBF bandwidth σ\sigma) tune() placeholders; grid search explore combinations.

title: tidymodels workflow with psvr
code: wf <- workflow() |>   add_recipe(rec) |>   add_model(spec)

title: tidymodels workflow with psvr
text: search 15-point Latin hypercube cost rbf_sigma values evaluate fold MAPE. rbf_sigma search range set data-adaptively using rbf_sigma_psvr_data(), centred median pairwise distance normalised feature space. rmspe_lssvr() solves linear system, 75 fits complete seconds. Cross-validated MAPE candidate (lower better):
code: set.seed(2) folds <- vfold_cv(train, v = 5)  # Data-driven rbf_sigma range centred on median pairwise distance train_baked      <- rec |> prep() |> bake(new_data = train) rbf_sigma_custom <- rbf_sigma_psvr_data(train_baked |> select(-y))  wf_params <- extract_parameter_set_dials(wf) |>   update(rbf_sigma = rbf_sigma_custom)  tune_res <- tune_grid(   wf,   resamples  = folds,   grid       = 15,   param_info = wf_params,   metrics    = metric_set(mape) ) collect_metrics(tune_res)[, c("cost", "rbf_sigma", "mean", "std_err")] #> # A tibble: 15 × 4 #>        cost rbf_sigma  mean std_err #>       <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl> #>  1    0.25      4.78  42.0   1.07   #>  2    0.453     0.478 35.0   1.13   #>  3    0.820     1.78  38.2   1.11   #>  4    1.49     12.8   42.0   1.07   #>  5    2.69      0.178 34.0   1.13   #>  6    4.88      0.665 16.9   0.538  #>  7    8.83      2.48  30.4   1.17   #>  8   16         9.23  41.8   1.13   #>  9   29.0       0.248 14.7   0.472  #> 10   52.5       0.923  6.18  0.118  #> 11   95.1       3.44  18.8   0.897  #> 12  172.       17.8   41.8   1.22   #> 13  312.        0.344  4.05  0.322  #> 14  565.        1.28   2.99  0.0743 #> 15 1024         6.65  20.0   0.951

code: best_params <- select_best(tune_res, metric = "mape") best_params #> # A tibble: 1 × 3 #>    cost rbf_sigma .config          #>   <dbl>     <dbl> <chr>            #> 1  565.      1.28 pre0_mod14_post0

text: last_fit() refits full training set chosen cost evaluates held-test data. Predictions test set: fitted workflow can also used directly new data:
code: final_wf  <- finalize_workflow(wf, best_params) final_fit <- last_fit(final_wf, split, metrics = metric_set(mape))  collect_metrics(final_fit) #> # A tibble: 1 × 4 #>   .metric .estimator .estimate .config         #>   <chr>   <chr>          <dbl> <chr>           #> 1 mape    standard        3.14 pre0_mod0_post0 preds <- collect_predictions(final_fit) head(preds[, c(".row", "y", ".pred")]) #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>    .row     y .pred #>   <int> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1     3  5.99  6.05 #> 2     4  6.20  6.14 #> 3     5  4.69  4.88 #> 4     6  1.96  2.02 #> 5     8  6.70  6.77 #> 6     9  3.93  4.03 new_obs <- data.frame(x1 = c(0, 1, -2), x2 = c(0, 1, 2)) predict(extract_workflow(final_fit), new_data = new_obs) #> # A tibble: 3 × 1 #>   .pred #>   <dbl> #> 1  1.97 #> 2  3.53 #> 3  8.13
