text: tidymodels layer wraps psvr_rmspe object (returned rmspe_lssvr()). Extract use print() coef() directly.
code: # extract_fit_engine() unwraps the parsnip/workflow layer to the raw psvr object psvr_fit <- extract_fit_engine(extract_workflow(final_fit)) print(psvr_fit) #>  #> LS-SVR with RMSPE loss  [psvr_rmspe] #>  #>   Kernel:        RBF (sigma = 1.28306) #>   Gamma:         565.294 #>   Training obs.: 150 cf <- coef(psvr_fit) # alpha: N dual variables; weight each training point in f(x) = sum_k alpha_k K(x_k, x) + b # b:     bias / intercept term # X_sv:  all N training inputs (LS-SVR has no sparsity — every training point contributes) cat(sprintf("b = %.4f  |  alpha range: [%.4f, %.4f]\n",             cf$b, min(cf$alpha), max(cf$alpha))) #> b = 12.6350  |  alpha range: [-18.4036, 21.9087]

title: When to Use Percentage-Error SVR
text: Standard regression models — including classical SVR — minimise absolute-error losses MSE MAE. losses scale-dependent: error 5 units treated identically whether target 10 10,000. many practical settings, however, matters relative error. forecast 5% 200 MPa concrete specimen equivalent practical terms one 5% 20 MPa specimen — even though absolute errors differ factor ten. psvr package provides four SVR variants optimise percentage-error losses directly: Model 1 (psvr_mape_rbf): ε\varepsilon-SVR MAPE loss Model 2 (psvr_mape_sym_rbf): symmetric-kernel extension Model 1 Model 3 (psvr_rmspe_rbf): LS-SVR RMSPE loss Model 4 (psvr_rmspe_sym_rbf): symmetric-kernel extension Model 3 article asks concrete question: dataset relative accuracy matters, models optimise percentage error directly outperform models optimise absolute error? Note psvr universal replacement standard SVR. models require strictly positive targets best suited settings : () target spans wide range scales, (b) domain specifications stated percentage terms, (c) models trained different units datasets need compared fairly.

title: When to Use Percentage-Error SVR
code: library(tidyverse) library(tidymodels) library(psvr) library(modeldata) library(future)  tidymodels_prefer() theme_set(theme_bw())

title: When to Use Percentage-Error SVR
text: concrete dataset (Yeh, 1998) contains 1,030 observations concrete mixture compositions resulting compressive strength curing. target — compressive strength MPa — strictly positive spans roughly 2.3 82.6 MPa, range 35-fold. wide scale variation makes natural candidate percentage-error evaluation.  Compressive strength spans wide range, motivating relative error metrics. distribution right-skewed long upper tail. dataset? 35-fold range compressive strength means absolute-error loss weights high-strength specimens far heavily low-strength ones. model optimised MSE can achieve low aggregate error fitting high-strength region well relatively inaccurate low-strength concrete — may structurally critical regime.

title: When to Use Percentage-Error SVR
text: four models use RBF kernel. symmetry parameter = 1 (even symmetry) fixed engine argument — encodes assumption regression function satisfies f(𝐱)=f(−𝐱)f(\mathbf{x}) = f(-\mathbf{x}), reasonable centering via step_normalize. Hyperparameter search ranges psvr package supplies custom dials parameters appropriate defaults percentage-error models: svm_margin → margin_percentage(): default range [1, 20] percentage units — manual override needed. cost → cost_psvr(): default range [−2, 10] log₂ scale (~0.25 1,024) — manual override needed. rbf_sigma → rbf_sigma_psvr(): default range [−3, 1] log₁₀ scale. range adjusted using median-distance heuristic via sigma_heuristic() — see code .
code: spec_lm  <- linear_reg() |>   set_engine("lm")  spec_svm <- svm_rbf(cost = tune(), rbf_sigma = tune()) |>   set_engine("kernlab") |>   set_mode("regression")  spec_rf  <- rand_forest(mtry = tune(), trees = 500, min_n = tune()) |>   set_engine("ranger") |>   set_mode("regression")  spec_xgb <- boost_tree(   trees     = 500,   tree_depth = tune(),   learn_rate = tune(),   loss_reduction = tune() ) |>   set_engine("xgboost") |>   set_mode("regression") spec_m1 <- psvr_mape_rbf(   cost       = tune(),   svm_margin = tune(),   rbf_sigma  = tune() ) |>   set_engine("psvr")  spec_m2 <- psvr_mape_sym_rbf(   cost       = tune(),   svm_margin = tune(),   rbf_sigma  = tune() ) |>   set_engine("psvr")  spec_m3 <- psvr_rmspe_rbf(   cost      = tune(),   rbf_sigma = tune() ) |>   set_engine("psvr")  spec_m4 <- psvr_rmspe_sym_rbf(   cost      = tune(),   rbf_sigma = tune() ) |>   set_engine("psvr") # Compute data-driven rbf_sigma parameter from the normalised training predictors train_baked      <- rec_base |> prep() |> bake(new_data = train) rbf_sigma_custom <- rbf_sigma_psvr_data(   train_baked |> select(-compressive_strength) )  r <- dials::range_get(rbf_sigma_custom, original = FALSE) cat(sprintf("rbf_sigma range: [%.3f, %.3f]\n", r$lower, r$upper)) rbf_sigma range: [-0.433, 1.567]

code: wf_set <- workflow_set(   preproc = list(base = rec_base),   models  = list(     lm           = spec_lm,     svm_rbf      = spec_svm,     rf           = spec_rf,     xgb          = spec_xgb,     m1_mape      = spec_m1,     m2_mape_sym  = spec_m2,     m3_rmspe     = spec_m3,     m4_rmspe_sym = spec_m4   ) ) |>   psvr_option_add(train_baked |> select(-compressive_strength))  wf_set # A workflow set/tibble: 8 × 4   wflow_id          info             option    result   <chr>             <list>           <list>    <list> 1 base_lm           <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[0]> <list [0]> 2 base_svm_rbf      <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[0]> <list [0]> 3 base_rf           <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[0]> <list [0]> 4 base_xgb          <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[0]> <list [0]> 5 base_m1_mape      <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[1]> <list [0]> 6 base_m2_mape_sym  <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[1]> <list [0]> 7 base_m3_rmspe     <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[1]> <list [0]> 8 base_m4_rmspe_sym <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[1]> <list [0]>

MAPE vs. RMSE trade-best configuration workflow. Models lower-left quadrant best metrics.
code: rank_res <- rank_results(tune_res, rank_metric = "mape", select_best = TRUE)  rank_res |>   filter(.metric == "mape") |>   mutate(     wflow_id = fct_reorder(wflow_id, mean),     family   = case_when(       str_detect(wflow_id, "m1|m2|m3|m4") ~ "psvr",       str_detect(wflow_id, "svm")          ~ "SVM (kernlab)",       str_detect(wflow_id, "rf")           ~ "Random Forest",       str_detect(wflow_id, "xgb")          ~ "XGBoost",       TRUE                                  ~ "Linear"     )   ) |>   ggplot(aes(x = mean, y = wflow_id, colour = family, shape = family)) +   geom_point(size = 3) +   geom_errorbarh(     aes(xmin = mean - 1.96 * std_err, xmax = mean + 1.96 * std_err),     height = 0.25   ) +   scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +   labs(     x        = "Cross-validated MAPE (%)",     y        = NULL,     colour   = "Model family",     shape    = "Model family",     title    = "Model comparison: cross-validated MAPE",     subtitle = "Points show best result per workflow; bars show ±1.96 SE"   ) rank_res |>   filter(.metric == "mape") |>   select(rank, wflow_id, mean, std_err, n) |>   rename(     Rank     = rank,     Workflow = wflow_id,     MAPE     = mean,     SE       = std_err,     Folds    = n   ) |>   mutate(across(c(MAPE, SE), \(x) round(x, 2))) |>   knitr::kable(caption = "Cross-validated MAPE — best configuration per workflow") best_mape <- rank_results(tune_res, rank_metric = "mape", select_best = TRUE) |>   filter(.metric == "mape") |>   select(wflow_id, mape = mean)  best_rmse <- rank_results(tune_res, rank_metric = "rmse", select_best = TRUE) |>   filter(.metric == "rmse") |>   select(wflow_id, rmse = mean)  tradeoff <- left_join(best_mape, best_rmse, by = "wflow_id") |>   mutate(     family = case_when(       str_detect(wflow_id, "m1|m2|m3|m4") ~ "psvr",       str_detect(wflow_id, "svm")          ~ "SVM (kernlab)",       str_detect(wflow_id, "rf")           ~ "Random Forest",       str_detect(wflow_id, "xgb")          ~ "XGBoost",       TRUE                                  ~ "Linear"     ),     label = str_remove(wflow_id, "^base_")   )  tradeoff |>   ggplot(aes(x = rmse, y = mape, colour = family, label = label)) +   geom_point(size = 3.5, alpha = 0.85) +   ggrepel::geom_text_repel(size = 3, max.overlaps = 20) +   scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +   labs(     x        = "Cross-validated RMSE (MPa)",     y        = "Cross-validated MAPE (%)",     colour   = "Model family",     title    = "MAPE vs. RMSE trade-off",     subtitle = "Each point = best hyperparameter config for that workflow"   )

title: psvr — Percentage-Error SVR
text: psvr implements four support vector regression models derived unified mathematical framework percentage-error loss functions. Classical SVR minimises absolute-error losses (MAE, MSE), scale-dependent: error 1 unit negligible target 1 000 critical 2. psvr addresses optimising MAPE RMSPE directly, making well-suited forecasting tasks targets strictly positive relative accuracy matters. models require strictly positive targets (y > 0).

title: psvr — Percentage-Error SVR

title: psvr — Percentage-Error SVR
text: full worked example Boston Housing dataset — including 70/30 train–test split, feature standardisation, comparison linear baseline — see vignette("getting-started", package = "psvr").
code:
library(psvr)  # Synthetic dataset: even function (f(-x) = f(x)), targets strictly positive set.seed(42) n  <- 100 X  <- matrix(rnorm(n * 2), n, 2) y  <- 2 + X[, 1]^2 + 0.5 * X[, 2]^2 + rnorm(n, sd = 0.1)  tr <- 1:70;  te <- 71:100 X_tr <- X[tr, ];  y_tr <- y[tr] X_te <- X[te, ];  y_te <- y[te]  # Standardise features using training-set statistics (important for RBF) col_mean <- colMeans(X_tr);  col_sd <- apply(X_tr, 2, sd) X_tr_s   <- scale(X_tr, col_mean, col_sd) X_te_s   <- scale(X_te, col_mean, col_sd)  K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1)  # Model 1 — ε-SVR with MAPE fit1  <- mape_svr(X_tr_s, y_tr, kernel = K, C = 0.5, eps = 5) pred1 <- predict(fit1, X_te_s)  # Model 2 — Symmetric ε-SVR with MAPE  (a = 1: even-function prior) fit2  <- mape_sym_svr(X_tr_s, y_tr, kernel = K, C = 0.5, eps = 5, a = 1) pred2 <- predict(fit2, X_te_s)  # Model 3 — LS-SVR with RMSPE fit3  <- rmspe_lssvr(X_tr_s, y_tr, kernel = K, gamma = 100) pred3 <- predict(fit3, X_te_s)  # Model 4 — Symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE  (a = 1: even-function prior) fit4  <- rmspe_sym_lssvr(X_tr_s, y_tr, kernel = K, gamma = 100, a = 1) pred4 <- predict(fit4, X_te_s)  mape <- function(y, yhat) mean(abs(y - yhat) / y) * 100  cat(sprintf("Model 1 (MAPE e-SVR):       MAPE = %.2f%%
", mape(y_te, pred1))) cat(sprintf("Model 2 (MAPE sym e-SVR):   MAPE = %.2f%%
", mape(y_te, pred2))) cat(sprintf("Model 3 (RMSPE LS-SVR):     MAPE = %.2f%%
", mape(y_te, pred3))) cat(sprintf("Model 4 (RMSPE sym LS-SVR): MAPE = %.2f%%
", mape(y_te, pred4))) #> Model 1 (MAPE e-SVR):       MAPE = 3.59% #> Model 2 (MAPE sym e-SVR):   MAPE = 6.04% #> Model 3 (RMSPE LS-SVR):     MAPE = 3.76% #> Model 4 (RMSPE sym LS-SVR): MAPE = 5.79%

text: Returns quant_param whose search range scales var(y) * N, standard heuristic LS-SVR regularisation parameter Γ (Suykens et al. 2002, Least Squares Support Vector Machines, §3.1.3).  log2 scale, lower bound -2 (.e. Γ ≥ 0.25) upper bound log2(var(y) * N) + width_log2.

title: Data-driven cost range for LS-SVR psvr models — cost_psvr_ls_data
code: cost_psvr_ls_data(y, n = length(y), width_log2 = 4)

title: Data-driven cost range for LS-SVR psvr models — cost_psvr_ls_data
text: y Numeric vector strictly positive training targets. n Sample size.  Default length(y). width_log2 Scalar giving half-width (log2 units) added log2(var(y) * n) set upper bound. Default 4 (≈16× headroom).  Negative values accepted warning, since resulting upper bound falls var(y) * n heuristic unlikely useful.

title: Data-driven cost range for LS-SVR psvr models — cost_psvr_ls_data
text: quant_param dials object.

title: Data-driven cost range for LS-SVR psvr models — cost_psvr_ls_data
text: default width_log2 = 4, upper bound covers typical optimum within ~2 orders magnitude benchmark datasets.  Boston Housing (var(medv) ≈ 84.6, N_train = 404 80/20 split), gives upper bound 2^19.06 ≈ 5.4 × 10⁵, two decades published optimum Γ ≈ 1.7 × 10⁴ — comfortable headroom Bayesian optimisation without boundary trapping.  static cost_psvr() range [-2, 10] (.e. Γ ≤ 1024) underestimates decade. Use function m3 (LS-SVR) m4 (symmetric LS-SVR) workflows.  Stick cost_psvr() m1/m2 (ε-SVR), cost maps C typical optima lie [10, 100].

title: Data-driven cost range for LS-SVR psvr models — cost_psvr_ls_data
code: cost_psvr_ls_data(c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50)) #> Cost (quantitative) #> Transformer: log-2 [1e-100, Inf] #> Range (transformed scale): [-2, 14.3]

what: Compute a kernel matrix between two sets of points — kernel_matrix
title: Compute a kernel matrix between two sets of points — kernel_matrix

title: Compute a kernel matrix between two sets of points — kernel_matrix
code: kernel_matrix(K, X1, X2 = X1)

title: Compute a kernel matrix between two sets of points — kernel_matrix
text: K kernel function make_kernel(). X1 Numeric matrix one observation per row (n1 × p). X2 Numeric matrix one observation per row (n2 × p). Defaults X1, giving square training kernel matrix Ω.

title: Compute a kernel matrix between two sets of points — kernel_matrix
text: Numeric matrix size n1 × n2.

title: Create a kernel function — make_kernel
text: Returns closure K(xi, xj) suitable use four psvr model fitting functions. returned function accepts numeric vectors sign, required symmetric models (Models 2 4) evaluate K(xk, -xl).

title: Create a kernel function — make_kernel
code: make_kernel(   type = c("rbf", "linear", "polynomial"),   sigma = 1,   degree = 3L,   coef0 = 1 )

title: Create a kernel function — make_kernel
text: type Kernel type: "rbf", "linear", "polynomial". sigma Bandwidth RBF kernel, sigma > 0 (default 1). degree Integer degree polynomial kernel, degree >= 1 (default 3). coef0 Constant term polynomial kernel (default 1).

title: Create a kernel function — make_kernel
text: function K(xi, xj) xi xj numeric vectors length, returning scalar kernel evaluation.

title: Create a kernel function — make_kernel
text: three supported kernels : RBF: K(xi, xj) = exp(-‖xi - xj‖² / (2 * sigma²)) Linear: K(xi, xj) = xi · xj Polynomial: K(xi, xj) = (xi · xj + coef0)^degree RBF even-degree polynomial kernels satisfy Assumption 3 paper (kernel symmetry), making compatible symmetric models. linear kernel odd-degree polynomial kernels satisfy Assumption 3 used mape_sym_svr() rmspe_sym_lssvr().

title: Create a kernel function — make_kernel
code: K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 0.5) K(c(1, 2), c(3, 4)) #> [1] 1.125352e-07  K_lin <- make_kernel("linear") K_lin(c(1, 0), c(0, 1)) #> [1] 0  K_poly <- make_kernel("polynomial", degree = 2, coef0 = 1) K_poly(c(1, 2), c(3, 4)) #> [1] 144

what: Fit epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — mape_svr
title: Fit epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — mape_svr
text: Solves quadratic program derived Theorem 1 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026) via osqp. dual expressed variables u = [α; α*] ∈ R^{2N} :

title: Fit epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — mape_svr
code: mape_svr(X, y, kernel, C, eps, solver = c("smo", "osqp"), tol = 1e-05)

title: Fit epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — mape_svr
text: X Numeric matrix training inputs, one observation per row (N × p). y Numeric vector training targets, length N. Must satisfy y > 0. kernel kernel function created make_kernel(). C Regularization parameter C > 0. eps Insensitivity tube half-width ε ≥ 0 (percentage units, .e., tube ε% target). solver Backend solves dual QP.  "smo" (default) uses internal libsvm-style SMO solver third-party dependency; "osqp" delegates osqp package (becomes soft requirement). backends solve QP return support vectors bias numerical tolerance. tol Threshold |βk| treated zero selecting support vectors free support vectors (default 1e-5).

title: Fit epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — mape_svr
text: object class "psvr_mape", list components: beta Numeric vector non-zero dual differences βk (length equal number support vectors). b Numeric scalar bias term. X_sv Numeric matrix support vector inputs. y_sv Numeric vector support vector targets. kernel kernel function used. C regularization parameter C. eps ε value used. n_train Number training observations. p_train Number training features (columns).

title: Fit epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — mape_svr
text: P = [Ω, -Ω; -Ω, Ω] (2N × 2N, upper triangular passed osqp) q = [y(ε/100 − 1); y(1 + ε/100)] Equality: [1ᵀ, −1ᵀ] u = 0 Box: 0 ≤ αk ≤ 100C/yk, 0 ≤ αk* ≤ 100C/yk solving, βk = αk − αk* support vectors |βk| > tol retained.

title: Fit epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — mape_svr
code: X <- matrix(1:6, ncol = 2) y <- c(2.1, 3.8, 6.2) K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1) fit <- mape_svr(X, y, kernel = K, C = 10, eps = 5) predict(fit, X) #> [1] 2.207585 3.988709 5.888707

what: Fit symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — mape_sym_svr
title: Fit symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — mape_sym_svr
text: Solves quadratic program derived Theorem 2 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026) via osqp. symmetry constraint f(x) = ·f(-x) enforced replacing kernel Ks(xi, xj) = K(xi, xj) + ·K(xi, -xj).

title: Fit symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — mape_sym_svr
code: mape_sym_svr(   X,   y,   kernel,   C,   eps,   a = 1,   solver = c("smo", "osqp"),   tol = 1e-05 )

title: Fit symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — mape_sym_svr
text: X Numeric matrix training inputs, one observation per row (N × p). y Numeric vector training targets, length N. Must satisfy y > 0. kernel kernel function created make_kernel(). C Regularization parameter C > 0. eps Insensitivity tube half-width ε ≥ 0 (percentage units). Symmetry parameter: 1 even symmetry f(x) = f(-x), -1 odd symmetry f(x) = -f(-x). solver Backend solves dual QP.  "smo" (default) uses internal libsvm-style SMO solver third-party dependency; "osqp" delegates osqp package.  backends solve QP return support vectors bias numerical tolerance. solver = "smo", ½ factor symmetric kernel Ks lives effective Omega = ½·Ks passed solver, prediction-time ½ symmetric representer theorem unchanged. tol Threshold |βk| treated zero (default 1e-5).

title: Fit symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — mape_sym_svr
text: object class "psvr_mape_sym", list components: beta Numeric vector non-zero dual differences βk. b Numeric scalar bias term. X_sv Numeric matrix support vector inputs. y_sv Numeric vector support vector targets. kernel kernel function used. C regularization parameter C. eps ε value used. symmetry parameter. n_train Number training observations. p_train Number training features (columns).

title: Fit symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — mape_sym_svr
text: dual variables u = [α; α*] ∈ R^{2N} : P = ½ · [Ks, -Ks; -Ks, Ks] osqp's ½ uᵀPu evaluates ¼ βᵀKsβ, matching −¼ coefficient Theorem 2. q = [y(ε/100 − 1); y(1 + ε/100)] (identical Model 1) Equality: [1ᵀ, −1ᵀ] u = 0 Box: 0 ≤ αk ≤ 100C/yk, 0 ≤ αk* ≤ 100C/yk (identical Model 1) Note: Ks = Ω + ·Ω* carries ½ factor . ½ lives P osqp's internal ½ produces required ¼ overall. Contrast rmspe_sym_lssvr() (Model 4), uses sym_kernel_matrix() returning ½(Ω + ·Ω*) = Ωs. kernel must satisfy Assumption 3 paper; see make_kernel().

title: Fit symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — mape_sym_svr
code: X <- matrix(1:6, ncol = 2) y <- c(2.1, 3.8, 6.2) K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1) fit <- mape_sym_svr(X, y, kernel = K, C = 10, eps = 5, a = 1) predict(fit, X) #> [1] 2.207585 3.988709 5.888707

what: Fit wrappers for parsnip engine dispatch — psvr-fit-wrappers
title: Fit wrappers for parsnip engine dispatch — psvr-fit-wrappers
text: Low-level bridge functions called parsnip fitting psvr model specs. intended direct use; call mape_svr(), mape_sym_svr(), rmspe_lssvr(), rmspe_sym_lssvr() directly instead.

code: psvr_mape_rbf_fit(x, y, C, eps, rbf_sigma = 1, tol = 1e-05)  psvr_mape_poly_fit(x, y, C, eps, degree = 3L, scale_factor = 1, tol = 1e-05)  psvr_mape_linear_fit(x, y, C, eps, tol = 1e-05)  psvr_mape_sym_rbf_fit(   x,   y,   C,   eps,   rbf_sigma = 1,   sym_type = "even",   tol = 1e-05 )  psvr_mape_sym_poly_fit(   x,   y,   C,   eps,   degree = 3L,   scale_factor = 1,   a = 1L,   tol = 1e-05 )  psvr_mape_sym_linear_fit(x, y, C, eps, a = 1L, tol = 1e-05)  psvr_rmspe_rbf_fit(x, y, gamma, rbf_sigma = 1, precondition = "auto")  psvr_rmspe_poly_fit(   x,   y,   gamma,   degree = 3L,   scale_factor = 1,   precondition = "auto" )  psvr_rmspe_linear_fit(x, y, gamma, precondition = "auto")  psvr_rmspe_sym_rbf_fit(   x,   y,   gamma,   rbf_sigma = 1,   sym_type = "even",   precondition = "auto" )  psvr_rmspe_sym_poly_fit(   x,   y,   gamma,   degree = 3L,   scale_factor = 1,   a = 1L,   precondition = "auto" )  psvr_rmspe_sym_linear_fit(x, y, gamma, a = 1L, precondition = "auto")

title: Fit wrappers for parsnip engine dispatch — psvr-fit-wrappers
text: x Numeric predictor matrix (parsnip matrix interface). y Numeric outcome vector (strictly positive). C Regularization parameter MAPE models (see mape_svr()). eps Epsilon tube half-width MAPE models. rbf_sigma RBF bandwidth σ > 0. tol Solver zero-threshold (see mape_svr()). degree Polynomial degree ≥ 1. scale_factor Polynomial constant term (coef₀). sym_type Symmetry type ("even" "odd") symmetric models; translated = 1L = -1L calling solver. gamma Regularization parameter RMSPE models (see rmspe_lssvr()). precondition Optional symmetric rescaling preconditioner RMSPE LS-SVR fitters. Forwarded rmspe_lssvr() / rmspe_sym_lssvr(); see functions accepted values semantics.

title: Fit LS-SVR with RMSPE loss (Model 3) — rmspe_lssvr
text: YΓ = diag(y₁²/Γ, …, yN²/Γ) added diagonal Ω. target dynamic range ρ = max(y) / min(y) large, diagonal YΓ = diag(yₖ²/Γ) varies O(ρ²), making Ω + YΓ ill-conditioned. Remark 17 companion paper derives symmetric rescaling preconditioner P = diag(1/yₖ) via change variable α = P ᾱ (.e. αₖ = ᾱₖ / yₖ). Multiplying inner block bordered system P left gives (P Ω P + Γ⁻¹·) ᾱ = P y − b · P 1 = 1 − b · P 1, constant-diagonal regularization Γ⁻¹ · independent yₖ. constraint 1ᵀ α = 0 becomes (P 1)ᵀ ᾱ = 0, bordered system used preconditioned solver Recovery α = ᾱ / y (elementwise division). bias b constraint multiplier systems. strict change variable: exact arithmetic preconditioned unconditioned solvers produce identical predictions. purpose preserve solver accuracy finite floating-point precision ρ large; moderate ρ two paths agree within machine epsilon. Use precondition = "auto" (default) typical workloads, "never" legacy behaviour, custom numeric threshold fine-grained control. chosen behaviour recorded precondition_applied.

title: psvr 0.0.1
text: Added optional preconditioner LS-SVR variants (rmspe_lssvr(), rmspe_sym_lssvr()) via precondition argument. Accepted values: “always”, “never”, “auto” (default; activates max(y)/min(y) > 10), numeric threshold. preconditioner mathematically exact change variable improves numerical conditioning large target dynamic ranges without changing predictions exact arithmetic. Returned model objects now include precondition_applied (logical) diagnostic transparency. print method displays field TRUE.

title: psvr 0.0.1
text: Updated @details LS-SVR fitters bordered-system formulation recovery formula α_k = α̃_k / y_k.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9005
text: cost_psvr_ls_data(y, n, width_log2) — data-driven cost range LS-SVR psvr models (m3, m4). Default upper bound scales log2(var(y) * N) + width_log2 (default width_log2 = 4), standard heuristic LS-SVR regularisation parameter Γ (Suykens et al. 2002, Least Squares Support Vector Machines, §3.1.3). Use instead cost_psvr() m3/m4 workflows; static [-2, 10] log2 range underestimates Γ benchmark datasets (Boston Housing optimum Γ ≈ 1.7 × 10⁴, vs. cost_psvr() upper bound 2^10 = 1024). psvr_option_add_cost_ls(wf_set, y, ...) — convenience wrapper applies cost_psvr_ls_data() every LS-SVR psvr workflow workflow set (whose wflow_id matches m3|m4), analogous psvr_option_add() rbf_sigma.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9004
text: sym_type now tunable parsnip argument psvr_mape_sym_rbf() psvr_rmspe_sym_rbf(). Pass sym_type = tune() let CV select even (= 1) odd (= -1) symmetry automatically. Engine default = 1L removed symmetric RBF specs; symmetry type now controlled exclusively sym_type model argument.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9003
text: New function rbf_sigma_psvr_data(): combines sigma_heuristic() rbf_sigma_psvr() single call, returning dials parameter data-driven search range centred median pairwise distance. New function psvr_option_add(): applies option_add() every psvr workflow (whose wflow_id matches m1|m2|m3|m4) workflow set simultaneously, replacing rbf_sigma parameter data-driven one. Replaces four individual option_add() calls typical workflows. Symmetric model specs (psvr_mape_sym_*, psvr_rmspe_sym_*): even symmetry (= 1L) now default engine argument. Calling set_engine("psvr") without = 1L now produces result — action required existing code already passed = 1L. four core fitting functions now emit warning N > 2000 remind users O(n²) kernel matrix may expensive. y > 0 input check four core fitting functions now reports number offending values minimum, making easier diagnose data issues.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9002
text: New function margin_percentage(): dials parameter epsilon tube half-width MAPE models, expressed percentage units default range [1, 20]. Replaces absolute-unit dials::svm_margin() 6 MAPE specs. New function sigma_heuristic(): returns median pairwise Euclidean distance predictor matrix — standard data-driven starting point RBF kernel bandwidth (Schölkopf & Smola, 2002). New function rbf_sigma_psvr(): dials parameter RBF kernel bandwidth finalize hook auto-sets search range using sigma_heuristic() training data available. Replaces dials::rbf_sigma() RBF specs. New function cost_psvr(): dials parameter regularisation parameter range [-2, 10] log2 scale (approx. 0.25 1024), wider dials::cost() accommodate larger values typically needed LS-SVR models. Replaces dials::cost() 12 specs. rbf_sigma_psvr(): removed non-functional finalize hook. search range must set manually using sigma_heuristic() option_add(). See ?rbf_sigma_psvr recommended workflow.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9001
text: Expanded parsnip integration 4 12 model specs following tidymodels pattern used svm_rbf(), svm_poly(), svm_linear(): psvr_mape_rbf(), psvr_mape_poly(), psvr_mape_linear() — Model 1 specs. psvr_mape_sym_rbf(), psvr_mape_sym_poly(), psvr_mape_sym_linear() — Model 2 specs. psvr_rmspe_rbf(), psvr_rmspe_poly(), psvr_rmspe_linear() — Model 3 specs. psvr_rmspe_sym_rbf(), psvr_rmspe_sym_poly(), psvr_rmspe_sym_linear() — Model 4 specs. Kernel parameters now tunable parsnip args mapped existing dials params: rbf_sigma → dials::rbf_sigma(), degree → dials::degree(), scale_factor → dials::scale_factor(). kernel closure built inside fit wrapper longer appears parsnip layer. old single-spec API (psvr_mape(), psvr_mape_sym(), psvr_rmspe(), psvr_rmspe_sym()) removed. Migrate replacing, example, psvr_rmspe(cost = tune()) |> set_engine("psvr", kernel = K) psvr_rmspe_rbf(cost = tune(), rbf_sigma = 1) |> set_engine("psvr").

title: psvr 0.0.0.9001
text: vignettes pkgdown articles updated new spec-based API. 12 new smoke tests (fit + predict) specs tests/testthat/test-parsnip.R. pkgdown articles reorganised three named groups: Get Started, Case Studies, Technical Notes.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9000

title: psvr 0.0.0.9000
text: mape_svr() — epsilon-SVR MAPE loss (Model 1). Solves dual QP via osqp per-sample box constraints |βk| ≤ 100C/yₖ. mape_sym_svr() — symmetric epsilon-SVR MAPE loss (Model 2). Enforces f(x) = ·f(-x) replacing kernel symmetric kernel Ks(xi, xj) = K(xi, xj) + ·K(xi, -xj). rmspe_lssvr() — LS-SVR RMSPE loss (Model 3). Solves (N+1)×(N+1) linear system directly via base::solve(). rmspe_sym_lssvr() — symmetric LS-SVR RMSPE loss (Model 4). linear system Model 3 symmetrized kernel matrix Ωs = ½(Ω + ·Ω*). four models derived unified percentage-error loss framework; see Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026) mathematical proofs.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9000
text: make_kernel() — factory returning kernel closure type ∈ {"rbf", "linear", "polynomial"}. RBF even-degree polynomial kernels satisfy symmetry assumption (Assumption 3) required Models 2 4.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9000
text: Four parsnip model specifications use within tidymodels workflows: psvr_mape() — spec Model 1; hyperparameters cost svm_margin. psvr_mape_sym() — spec Model 2; hyperparameters cost svm_margin. psvr_rmspe() — spec Model 3; hyperparameter cost (maps Γ). psvr_rmspe_sym() — spec Model 4; hyperparameter cost (maps Γ). kernel (symmetric models) symmetry parameter engine arguments supplied via set_engine("psvr", kernel = ..., = ...). Hyperparameters map dials::cost() dials::svm_margin() compatibility tune_grid().

text: 84 unit tests covering four models: kernel correctness, QP/linear-system solutions, support vector selection, bias recovery, predict consistency, input validation.
